<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *Define the term "principal source of optimization" in the context of a compiler. List any four such sources. State the property that all of them must satisfy.*

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A **principal source of optimization** is a recurring, identifiable pattern in the intermediate code (typically Three-Address Code) of a program that can be transformed by the compiler into an *equivalent but more efficient* form.

**Four principal sources:**
1. **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**
2. **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)**
3. **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)**
4. **Strength Reduction**

**Mandatory property:** Every optimization must be a **function-preserving transformation**, i.e., the program must produce the *same output* for every legal input both before and after the transformation.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks: Definition 1 + Four sources 1.5 + Property 0.5.

---

**Q2.** *Explain the difference between "Constant Folding" and "Constant Propagation" with suitable examples.*

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Constant Folding | Constant Propagation |
|--------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Evaluating a constant expression at compile time and replacing it with the result. | Substituting the value of a variable known to be a constant into all its uses. |
| **Trigger** | Both operands are literals. | A variable is assigned a literal and not redefined before use. |
| **Mechanism** | Direct arithmetic on literals. | Data-flow analysis (reaching definitions). |
| **Example** | $a = 3 + 4 \Rightarrow a = 7$ | $a = 5; b = a + 3 \Rightarrow a = 5; b = 5 + 3 \Rightarrow b = 8$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Both are usually applied in conjunction: propagation first, then folding.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** *Answer either Question A or Question B.*

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Explain the three principal sources of optimization ŌĆö Common Subexpression Elimination, Dead Code Elimination, and Loop-Invariant Code Motion ŌĆö with the data-flow analysis that justifies each. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Consider the following Three-Address Code segment. Apply ALL applicable principal optimizations in sequence. Show the intermediate code after each step and justify the legality of each transformation. (7 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(1)\;\; a = 2 \\
&(2)\;\; b = 4 \\
&(3)\;\; t_1 = a \times b \\
&(4)\;\; t_2 = t_1 + a \\
&(5)\;\; c = t_2 \\
&(6)\;\; d = c \times 2 \\
&(7)\;\; t_3 = a \times b \quad\quad \text{(redundant)} \\
&(8)\;\; e = t_3 + 5 \\
&(9)\;\; \text{if } d > e \text{ goto } L_1 \\
&(10)\;\; \text{return } 0 \\
&(11)\;\; L_1: \text{ return } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Model Solution for Q-A(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**1. Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) ŌĆö 2 marks**

CSE removes redundant computations of expressions whose operands are unchanged. It is justified by the **Available Expressions** data-flow framework. An expression $e$ is *available* at a program point $p$ if every path from the entry to $p$ computes $e$ last, and no operand of $e$ has been redefined after that computation.

**Equations:**
$$
\text{OUT}[B] = \bigcap_{P \in pred[B]} \text{IN}[P]; \quad
\text{IN}[B] = \text{USE}_B \cup (\text{OUT}[B] - \text{DEF}_B)
$$

**Transformation:** The first computation of $e$ is kept; subsequent ones are replaced with a reference to its target variable.

**2. Dead Code Elimination (DCE) ŌĆö 2 marks**

A statement is *dead* if the variable it defines is **not live** at that point. Liveness is established by the **Live Variable** data-flow framework (a backward flow analysis):

$$
\text{IN}[B] = \text{USE}[B] \cup (\text{OUT}[B] - \text{DEF}[B]); \quad
\text{OUT}[B] = \bigcup_{S \in succ[B]} \text{IN}[S]
$$

**Transformation:** Delete any statement whose LHS is not in $\text{IN}[B]$.

**3. Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) ŌĆö 3 marks**

A statement $S$ inside a natural loop $L$ is **loop-invariant** if every operand of $S$ is either a constant or has a *reaching definition outside $L$*. Such a statement is hoisted to the **pre-header** of the loop. The legality rests on the **dominance** property of the pre-header over the loop body and the fact that invariant operands are not redefined inside $L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** LICM is applied AFTER CSE and DCE; this is the canonical ordering.

---

### Model Solution for Q-A(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆö Constant Folding on lines (1), (2), (3), (4), (6):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&(1)\;\; t_1 = 2 \times 4 = 8 \\
&(2)\;\; t_2 = 8 + 2 = 10 \\
&(3)\;\; d = 10 \times 2 = 20 \\
&(4)\;\; e = 8 + 5 = 13 \quad (\text{via CSE then fold}) \\
&(5)\;\; \text{if } 20 > 13 \text{ goto } L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö CSE on line (7):** `t3 = a * b` was already computed as `t1 = 8` on line (3). Replace with `t1`.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Copy Propagation on line (5):** `c = t2` then `d = c * 2` becomes `d = t2 * 2`.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Dead Code Elimination:**
- Line (3) in original `c = t2` becomes dead because `c` is never used after the propagation. **Remove.**
- Line `if 20 > 13 goto L1` becomes `if true goto L1`.
- `return 0` is now **unreachable**. **Remove.**

**Step 5 ŌĆö Peephole / Jump-to-Jump:** `goto L1; L1: return 1` collapses to `return 1`.

**Final Optimized Code:**
$$
\text{return } 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The student must show the **intermediate** state after each of the 5 steps and explicitly justify each transformation. Award 1 mark per step + 2 marks for the final consolidated code.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Discuss "Strength Reduction" and "Induction Variable Elimination" as principal sources of loop optimization. Use a worked example. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For the following loop body in TAC, apply LICM, Strength Reduction, and Induction Variable Elimination. Show the transformed code and quantify the speedup. (7 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
&i = 0 \\
&L: \;\; t_1 = 4 \times i \\
&\quad\;\; A[t_1] = i \\
&\quad\;\; i = i + 1 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{if } i < 100 \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Model Solution for Q-B(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Strength Reduction (3 marks):** Replaces an expensive operation (e.g., multiplication) by an equivalent cheaper one (e.g., addition or shift). In loops, the canonical form is: if $t = a \times i$ where $i$ is an induction variable, replace the multiplication with a running addition initialized to $a$ and updated by $a$ each iteration.

**Induction Variable Elimination (4 marks):** An **induction variable** is one whose value on each iteration is a linear function of the iteration count: $x = x_0 + c \cdot k$. Multiple induction variables in the same family (linear combinations of one base variable) are redundant. The optimization removes all but one from the family, replacing uses with the base variable scaled appropriately.

**Worked example** (covered in detail in Section 3 of these notes). Final transformed loop:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&t_1 = 0;\; i = 0 \\
&L: \;\; A[t_1] = i \\
&\quad\;\; t_1 = t_1 + 4;\; i = i + 1 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{if } i < 100 \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quantification:** Original loop executes one multiplication + one addition per iteration. Transformed loop executes two additions per iteration. On a typical 5-stage pipeline where multiplication takes **3ŌĆō4 cycles** and addition takes **1 cycle**, the speedup per iteration is from ~5 cycles to ~2 cycles $\Rightarrow$ **~2.5├Ś speedup** in the loop body.

### Model Solution for Q-B(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

The example is identical to Q-B(a) but the student is required to show each step:

1. **Identify `i` as the base induction variable, `t1` as a derived induction variable** (related by $t_1 = 4i$).
2. **Strength-reduce `t1 = 4 * i`** to `t1 = t1 + 4` initialized before the loop.
3. **LICM:** No loop-invariant statement inside the loop body (the constant 4 is literal; the bound 100 is loop-invariant ŌĆö hoist to a `LIMIT` variable for clarity).
4. **Induction variable elimination:** `t1` is the only necessary induction variable; `i` is used only in the comparison and the increment. If we keep `t1` and the bound `LIMIT = 4 * 100 = 400` is loop-invariant (hoisted), we can eliminate `i` entirely.

**Final optimized loop:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&t_1 = 0;\; LIMIT = 400 \\
&L: \;\; A[t_1] = t_1 / 4 \\
&\quad\;\; t_1 = t_1 + 4 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{if } t_1 < LIMIT \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

If a small loop-unroll + reciprocal multiply is used for `t1 / 4`, this can be further reduced to a shift `t_1 >> 2`, giving an overall **~3├Ś speedup**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** 2 marks for identifying induction variables, 2 marks for LICM application, 2 marks for strength reduction, 1 mark for the final code and speedup estimate.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner's Notes):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the "function-preserving" condition.** Many students apply optimizations without stating the legality condition. *Always* write: *"This transformation preserves the program semantics because ..."* (1 mark reserved).
> 2. **Confusing CSE with copy propagation.** CSE eliminates *expression* redundancy; copy propagation eliminates *variable* redundancy. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Applying LICM without verifying the loop-invariance pre-condition.** If an operand is redefined inside the loop, hoisting is illegal. State the pre-condition explicitly.
> 4. **Order of optimizations matters.** The canonical order is: Constant Folding ŌåÆ Constant Propagation ŌåÆ CSE ŌåÆ Copy Propagation ŌåÆ DCE ŌåÆ LICM ŌåÆ Strength Reduction ŌåÆ IVE. Reversing this order (e.g., LICM before CSE) misses opportunities.
> 5. **Confusing "dead code" with "unreachable code."** Dead = result not used. Unreachable = block never executed. Both are removed, but by different analyses.
> 6. **Not showing intermediate IR.** Showing only the final code costs 2ŌĆō3 marks. Always show **each** step.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Principal Sources of Optimizations (KTU 2024)**
> 
> - **Definition:** A *principal source* is a recurring IR pattern enabling a semantics-preserving efficiency improvement.
> - **Function-preservation is mandatory** ŌĆö every optimization must be justified.
> - **Machine-independent sources** are: constant folding/propagation, algebraic simplification, CSE, copy propagation, DCE, LICM, strength reduction, IVE, inlining.
> - **Machine-dependent sources** are: register allocation, instruction scheduling, peephole optimization.
> - **Data-flow analyses** (Available Expressions, Reaching Definitions, Live Variables) are the *theoretical backbone* of CSE, DCE, and Copy Propagation.
> - **Loop optimizations** (LICM, Strength Reduction, IVE) dominate runtime gains for numerical code.
> - **Canonical order of optimization passes:** Folding ŌåÆ Propagation ŌåÆ CSE ŌåÆ CopyProp ŌåÆ DCE ŌåÆ LICM ŌåÆ Strength ŌåÆ IVE.
> - **Trade-offs:** Inlining (timeŌåæ, spaceŌåō), LICM (timeŌåō, spaceŌåō), strength reduction (timeŌåō, code size neutral).
> - **KTU favorite exam tip:** Be ready to optimize a 5ŌĆō10 line TAC segment showing **all 5 stages** with justification.
> - **The optimization triangle:** time, space, energy ŌĆö every pass must be understood in this triad.
> - **The dominance concept** underpins LICM's legality.
> - **Peephole optimizations** are local, simple, and powerful ŌĆö they catch what global analyses miss.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process State Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> A **Process State** is the current operational condition of a process at any given instant during its lifetime in the CPU scheduler's perspective. The OS kernel maintains a finite set of discrete states through which every process transitions as it moves from creation to termination. These states represent the status of a process with respect to system resources such as the **CPU**, **main memory**, and **I/O devices**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Operating Systems Module**, a process traverses through a well-defined **Process State Diagram (PSD)** that captures the dynamic lifecycle of program execution under multiprogramming environments.

### 1.2 The Five Canonical Process States (Standard Two-State Model Extended)

| State Name | Symbol | Operational Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **New** | `N` | Process is being created |
| **Ready** | `R` | Process is waiting to be assigned to a processor |
| **Running** | `X` | Instructions are being executed by the CPU |
| **Blocked / Waiting** | `B` | Process is waiting for some event (I/O completion) |
| **Exit / Terminated** | `E` | Process has finished execution |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Bank Teller Counter

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Bank Branch Analogy**
> Imagine you walk into a bank with three counters (analogous to a **single-core CPU**):
> - **New**: You just entered the bank and are filling the entry form (process creation in progress).
> - **Ready**: Your form is accepted; you are standing in the queue waiting for a free counter (waiting in the **Ready Queue**).
> - **Running**: You are at Counter 1 getting your transaction processed (CPU actively executing your process).
> - **Blocked**: While at the counter, the manager asks you to fetch a signature from the back office. You step aside and wait (waiting for **I/O or event completion**).
> - **Exit**: Your transaction is complete; you walk out of the bank (process termination).

Just as customers move between these conditions, the OS scheduler moves processes between states. The fundamental invariant is: **At any instant, exactly ONE process (on a single-core system) can be in the Running state**.

### 1.4 Key High-Yield Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know KTU Glossary**
> - **PCB (Process Control Block)**: Kernel data structure holding the state, registers, memory map, and accounting info.
> - **Dispatcher**: The module giving CPU control to the process selected by the short-term scheduler.
> - **Long-term Scheduler (Job Scheduler)**: Decides which jobs enter the Ready queue from the New state.
> - **Short-term Scheduler (CPU Scheduler)**: Decides which Ready process gets the CPU next.
> - **State Transition**: A directed edge in the PSD, triggered by an event (I/O, interrupt, scheduler dispatch).
> - **Voluntary Release**: When a process gives up the CPU (e.g., for I/O).
> - **Involuntary Preemption**: When the scheduler forcibly removes a process from the CPU (time quantum expiry).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-State Model (Foundational Baseline)

The simplest model uses only **Running** and **Not Running** states.

```
                    Dispatch
   [Not Running]  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║  [Running]
         Ō¢▓                          Ōöé
         Ōöé                          Ōöé
         ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ Exit / I/O ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```

**Limitations of the Two-State Model**:
1. Cannot distinguish between a process waiting for the CPU vs. waiting for I/O.
2. Inefficient scheduler design ŌĆö wakeups of I/O-blocked processes wrongly wake non-existent CPU-bound candidates.

### 2.2 The Five-State Process Model (KTU Standard)

This is the model emphasized in KTU 2024 examinations. The transitions are:

**Step 1 ŌĆö New ŌåÆ Ready**
- Trigger: Long-term scheduler admits the process.
- Action: PCB is initialized, memory allocated, process placed in Ready queue.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Ready ŌåÆ Running**
- Trigger: Short-term scheduler dispatches the process via the dispatcher.
- Action: CPU registers loaded from PCB; program counter (PC) and stack pointer (SP) restored.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Running ŌåÆ Ready**
- Trigger: Time quantum expiry (preemptive) or higher-priority process arrival.
- Action: CPU context saved back into PCB; process re-queued.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Running ŌåÆ Blocked**
- Trigger: Process issues a system call for I/O, or waits for an event/signal.
- Action: Process is moved to the appropriate wait queue; CPU is relinquished voluntarily.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Blocked ŌåÆ Ready**
- Trigger: I/O completion interrupt fires; the OS moves the process back to the Ready queue.
- Action: PCB state field updated; process becomes eligible for scheduling.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Running ŌåÆ Exit**
- Trigger: Process executes final statement or calls `exit()` system call, OR abnormally terminated (segfault).
- Action: Resources deallocated, PCB marked as zombie, parent notified via `wait()`.

### 2.3 The Seven-State Model (Extended with Suspended States)

When memory pressure occurs, the OS may **suspend** a blocked process to secondary storage:

| New State | Reason for Existence |
|---|---|
| **Ready-Suspended** | Process was in Ready but swapped out to free main memory |
| **Blocked-Suspended** | Process was Blocked and additionally swapped out |

> [!NOTE]
> **Suspension Mechanics**
> Suspended processes reside on disk. The OS uses a **medium-term scheduler** to manage swap-in/swap-out operations. This is critical in systems with constrained RAM (embedded systems, early Unix).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Process State Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Utilization** | $U_{CPU} = 1 - P_{idle}$ | Fraction of time CPU is busy | % |
| **Throughput** | $T_p = \dfrac{N_{completed}}{\Delta t}$ | Processes completed per unit time | processes/sec |
| **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $T_{TAT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | Total time in system | seconds |
| **Waiting Time (WT)** | $T_{WT} = T_{TAT} - T_{burst}$ | Time spent in Ready queue | seconds |
| **Response Time (RT)** | $T_{RT} = T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival}$ | Time to first CPU access | seconds |
| **Context Switch Overhead** | $T_{CS} = T_{save} + T_{load}$ | Time to swap processes on CPU | microseconds |
| **Degree of Multiprogramming** | $D_M = \vert Q_{ready} \vert + \vert Q_{running} \vert$ | Active processes in memory | dimensionless count |
| **Quantum Size Rule** | $q > T_{CS}$ | Time slice must exceed switch cost | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition (KTU Favorite)**
> For a system to be efficient, the time quantum $q$ must satisfy: $q \gg T_{CS}$. If $q$ approaches $T_{CS}$, the system wastes most of its time context-switching rather than executing.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linux Kernel**: Uses states like `TASK_RUNNING`, `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE`, `TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE`, `TASK_STOPPED`, `TASK_TRACED`.
- **RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks)**: Uses simplified state machines like `Ready`, `Running`, `Blocked`, `Suspended`.
- **Android Process States**: `Foreground`, `Visible`, `Service`, `Background`, `Cached` ŌĆö directly mapped from OS process states.
- **Cloud/Container Orchestration**: Kubernetes uses similar lifecycle states for pod scheduling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Waiting Time for Given Process States

Consider $n$ processes $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ with arrival times $a_i$ and CPU bursts $b_i$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total Turnaround Time Equation**

$$
T_{TAT}^{(i)} = T_{waiting}^{(i)} + b_i + T_{I/O}^{(i)}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expand the Waiting Time Term**

The waiting time itself decomposes into Ready-queue wait and re-entry waits:

$$
T_{waiting}^{(i)} = \sum_{j=1}^{k_i} T_{ready\_j}^{(i)}
$$

where $k_i$ is the number of times process $i$ entered the Ready queue.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Aggregate Across All Processes**

$$
\overline{T_{WT}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{waiting}^{(i)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert to Bound (KTU Standard Form)**

For a non-preemptive FCFS scheduler with no I/O:

$$
\overline{T_{WT}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} b_j \right) = \frac{(n-1) \cdot \overline{b}}{2}
$$

where $\overline{b}$ is the mean CPU burst length.

**Interpretation**: On average, a process waits for half the total CPU work done before it, demonstrating why shorter jobs suffer in FCFS.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: State Transitions vs. Causes

| Transition | Direction | Trigger Event | Scheduler Role | Volition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{New} \rightarrow \text{Ready}$ | Forward | Admission | Long-term | System |
| $\text{Ready} \rightarrow \text{Running}$ | Forward | Dispatch | Short-term | System |
| $\text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Ready}$ | Backward | Preemption / Yield | Short-term | Mixed |
| $\text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Blocked}$ | Side | I/O request / Wait | None | Voluntary |
| $\text{Blocked} \rightarrow \text{Ready}$ | Side | I/O complete interrupt | None | System |
| $\text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Exit}$ | Terminal | `exit()` / fault | None | Voluntary/Forced |
| $\text{Blocked} \rightarrow \text{Blocked-Suspended}$ | Side (out) | Memory pressure | Medium-term | System |

### 3.3 Worked Example: State Transition Trace

**Problem**: Three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ arrive at times $t = 0, 1, 2$ with CPU bursts $5, 3, 2$ ms respectively under **Non-Preemptive SJF** scheduling. Compute average waiting time.

**Step 1 ŌĆö List Remaining Bursts Over Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = 0 &: P_1 \text{ enters Ready, dispatched (only one)} \\
t = 5 &: P_1 \text{ exits. Available: } P_2 \text{ (burst 3)}, P_3 \text{ (burst 2)} \\
t = 5 &: \text{SJF picks } P_3 \text{ (shorter)} \\
t = 7 &: P_3 \text{ exits. } P_2 \text{ runs} \\
t = 10 &: P_2 \text{ exits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Per-Process Waiting Times**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{WT}(P_1) &= 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
T_{WT}(P_2) &= 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
T_{WT}(P_3) &= 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Average**

$$
\overline{T_{WT}} = \frac{0 + 4 + 3}{3} = \frac{7}{3} \approx 2.33 \text{ ms}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Context Switch Cost Bound

Given $n$ processes each with burst $b$ and quantum $q$, the number of context switches is approximately:

$$
N_{CS} \approx \frac{n \cdot b}{q}
$$

Total time lost to context switching:

$$
T_{lost} = N_{CS} \cdot T_{CS} = \frac{n \cdot b \cdot T_{CS}}{q}
$$

Fraction of CPU wasted:

$$
F_{wasted} = \frac{T_{lost}}{T_{total}} = \frac{T_{CS}}{q}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight**: To keep $F_{wasted} < 1\%$, the scheduler must enforce $q \geq 100 \cdot T_{CS}$. For $T_{CS} = 10\,\mu s$, this gives $q \geq 1\,ms$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Five-State Process State Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["NEW"] -->|Long Term Scheduler Admits| B["READY"]
    B -->|Short Term Scheduler Dispatches| C["RUNNING"]
    C -->|Time Quantum Expires or Preempted| B
    C -->|Issues I/O or Waits for Event| D["BLOCKED WAITING"]
    D -->|I/O Completion Interrupt| B
    C -->|Exit System Call or Fault| E["EXIT TERMINATED"]
    B -.->|Memory Pressure Medium Term Swap Out| F["READY SUSPENDED"]
    D -.->|Memory Pressure Medium Term Swap Out| G["BLOCKED SUSPENDED"]
    F -.->|Swapped In| B
    G -.->|I/O Done and Swapped In| D
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of State Management

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PROCESS P1 PCB"] -->|State Field| Q1["READY QUEUE"]
    P2["PROCESS P2 PCB"] -->|State Field| Q1
    P3["PROCESS P3 PCB"] -->|State Field| Q2["WAIT QUEUE I/O"]
    P4["PROCESS P4 PCB"] -->|State Field| Q3["SUSPENDED QUEUE"]
    LTS["LONG TERM SCHEDULER"] -->|Admits| Q1
    STS["SHORT TERM SCHEDULER"] -->|Selects| CP["CPU CORE"]
    MTS["MEDIUM TERM SCHEDULER"] -->|Swap In Out| Q3
    CP -->|Preempted| Q1
    CP -->|I/O Request| Q2
    CP -->|Exit| TP["TERMINATION POOL"]
    Q2 -->|Interrupt| Q1
```

### 4.3 State Transition Trigger Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Triggers["TRANSITION TRIGGERS"]
        T1["Admission by LTS"]
        T2["Dispatch by STS"]
        T3["Quantum Expiry"]
        T4["I/O System Call"]
        T5["I/O Completion Interrupt"]
        T6["Process Exit"]
        T7["Swap Out by MTS"]
        T8["Swap In by MTS"]
    end
    Triggers --> Engine["STATE TRANSITION ENGINE"]
    Engine --> Update["UPDATE PCB STATE FIELD"]
    Update --> Requeue["MOVE PCB TO NEW QUEUE"]
```

### 4.4 Process Lifecycle Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant OS as Operating System
    participant LTS as Long Term Scheduler
    participant STS as Short Term Scheduler
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant IO as I/O Device
    U->>OS: fork()
    OS->>OS: Create PCB
    OS->>LTS: Submit for admission
    LTS->>OS: Admit to Ready
    OS->>STS: Ready queue updated
    STS->>CPU: Dispatch process
    CPU->>OS: I/O request
    OS->>IO: Issue I/O
    OS->>STS: Move to Blocked queue
    IO->>OS: I/O complete
    OS->>STS: Move to Ready queue
    STS->>CPU: Dispatch again
    CPU->>OS: exit()
    OS->>U: Process terminated
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a process state. List the various states of a process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A process state is the current condition of a process that indicates its status with respect to the system resources, particularly the CPU. The various states of a process in the five-state model are: **New, Ready, Running, Blocked, and Exit**. Each state represents a specific phase in the lifecycle of the process, from creation through execution to termination.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Differentiate between Ready state and Blocked state of a process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Ready State | Blocked State |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Status** | Process is waiting for CPU | Process is waiting for I/O or event |
| **Queue** | Resides in Ready Queue | Resides in Event/Wait Queue |
| **Trigger to move** | Dispatch by scheduler | I/O completion interrupt |
| **Eligibility for CPU** | Immediately eligible | Not eligible until event occurs |
| **Example** | A process that just got preempted | A process waiting for disk read |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Drawing state diagrams without labeling transitions clearly with the event/trigger.
> 2. Confusing the **Blocked** state with the **Suspended** state (they are distinct concepts).
> 3. Forgetting to mention which scheduler triggers each transition (LTS vs STS vs MTS).
> 4. Missing the difference between **preemptive** and **non-preemptive** transitions.
> 5. Failing to relate state transitions to PCB updates.
> Always show both the diagram AND the trigger conditions for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the five-state process model with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of long-term, short-term, and medium-term schedulers in process state transitions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The five-state process model describes the lifecycle of a process using five distinct states: New, Ready, Running, Blocked, and Exit.

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N["NEW"] -->|Long Term Scheduler| R["READY"]
    R -->|Short Term Scheduler Dispatch| X["RUNNING"]
    X -->|Preemption or Yield| R
    X -->|I/O or Wait| B["BLOCKED"]
    B -->|I/O Complete| R
    X -->|Exit| E["EXIT"]
```

**Scheduler Roles (3 marks):**
- **Long-Term Scheduler (LTS)**: Controls the degree of multiprogramming by selecting jobs from New ŌåÆ Ready. Runs less frequently; optimizes the mix of I/O-bound and CPU-bound processes.
- **Short-Term Scheduler (STS)**: Selects the next process from Ready ŌåÆ Running. Runs very frequently (milliseconds); minimizes CPU idle time.
- **Medium-Term Scheduler (MTS)**: Handles swapping ŌĆö moves processes between main memory and secondary storage to ease memory pressure.

---

**(b)** A system has 4 processes with arrival times and burst times as follows. Compute the **average waiting time** and **average turnaround time** using **SJF (Non-Preemptive)** scheduling. Show the Gantt chart and state transitions of each process. **(7 Marks)**

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 |
| P2 | 2 | 4 |
| P3 | 4 | 1 |
| P4 | 5 | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Build Gantt Chart**

At $t = 0$, only P1 is in Ready ŌåÆ P1 runs from 0 to 7.
At $t = 7$, all processes have arrived. Remaining bursts: P2=4, P3=1, P4=4.
SJF picks P3 (burst=1) ŌåÆ runs from 7 to 8.
Then P2 and P4 tie at burst=4. By FCFS tiebreak, P2 ŌåÆ runs 8 to 12. P4 ŌåÆ 12 to 16.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt Chart: } &\vert\,P1\,\vert\,\vert\,P3\,\vert\,\vert\,P2\,\vert\,\vert\,P4\,\vert \\
& 0 \quad 7 \quad 8 \quad 12 \quad 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Compute Completion, TAT, WT**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CT}(P1) &= 7, \quad T_{TAT}(P1) = 7 - 0 = 7, \quad T_{WT}(P1) = 7 - 7 = 0 \\
T_{CT}(P2) &= 12, \quad T_{TAT}(P2) = 12 - 2 = 10, \quad T_{WT}(P2) = 10 - 4 = 6 \\
T_{CT}(P3) &= 8, \quad T_{TAT}(P3) = 8 - 4 = 4, \quad T_{WT}(P3) = 4 - 1 = 3 \\
T_{CT}(P4) &= 16, \quad T_{TAT}(P4) = 16 - 5 = 11, \quad T_{WT}(P4) = 11 - 4 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Compute Averages**

$$
\overline{T_{TAT}} = \frac{7 + 10 + 4 + 11}{4} = \frac{32}{4} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\overline{T_{WT}} = \frac{0 + 6 + 3 + 7}{4} = \frac{16}{4} = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): State Transitions Narrative**
- P1: New (t=0) ŌåÆ Ready (t=0) ŌåÆ Running (t=0) ŌåÆ Exit (t=7)
- P2: New (t=2) ŌåÆ Ready (t=2) ŌåÆ Running (t=8) ŌåÆ Exit (t=12)
- P3: New (t=4) ŌåÆ Ready (t=4) ŌåÆ Running (t=7) ŌåÆ Exit (t=8)
- P4: New (t=5) ŌåÆ Ready (t=5) ŌåÆ Running (t=12) ŌåÆ Exit (t=16)

**Final Answer:** $\overline{T_{WT}} = 4$ ms, $\overline{T_{TAT}} = 8$ ms.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **seven-state process model** including suspended states. Why is suspension needed in modern operating systems? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The seven-state model extends the five-state model by splitting the Ready and Blocked states into In-Memory and Suspended (swapped-out) variants.

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N["NEW"] -->|LTS Admits| RS["READY"]
    RS -->|STS Dispatch| X["RUNNING"]
    X -->|Preempt| RS
    X -->|I/O Wait| BS["BLOCKED"]
    BS -->|I/O Done| RS
    X -->|Exit| E["EXIT"]
    RS -.->|MTS Swap Out| RSS["READY SUSPENDED"]
    BS -.->|MTS Swap Out| BSS["BLOCKED SUSPENDED"]
    RSS -.->|MTS Swap In| RS
    BSS -.->|I/O Done then Swap In| BS
    BSS -.->|MTS Swap In First| RSS
```

**Need for Suspension (3 marks):**
1. **Memory Relief**: When main memory is saturated, less-active processes are moved to disk to free RAM for active processes.
2. **Power Efficiency**: Suspended processes consume no CPU and minimal power.
3. **System Responsiveness**: Brings in new processes faster by ensuring Ready queue is properly sized.
4. **Crash Recovery**: Suspended state preserves process context for later resumption after system crashes.

---

**(b)** Consider the following trace of process state transitions. Identify which scheduler is responsible for each transition and justify whether the transition is **preemptive** or **non-preemptive**. **(7 Marks)**

| Time (ms) | Process | Transition |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | P1 | New ŌåÆ Ready |
| 1 | P1 | Ready ŌåÆ Running |
| 5 | P1 | Running ŌåÆ Blocked |
| 6 | P2 | New ŌåÆ Ready |
| 7 | P1 | Blocked ŌåÆ Ready |
| 8 | P1 | Ready ŌåÆ Running |
| 12 | P1 | Running ŌåÆ Exit |

**Model Solution:**

**Analysis (1 mark per row, total 7 marks with reasoning):**

| Time | Transition | Scheduler | Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | New ŌåÆ Ready | Long-Term | Non-preemptive | Admission to system is voluntary/system-driven, not preemptive |
| 1 | Ready ŌåÆ Running | Short-Term | Non-preemptive | Initial dispatch (no running process to preempt) |
| 5 | Running ŌåÆ Blocked | None (Process action) | Non-preemptive | Voluntary I/O request ŌĆö process gives up CPU |
| 6 | New ŌåÆ Ready | Long-Term | Non-preemptive | P2 admitted while P1 is blocked |
| 7 | Blocked ŌåÆ Ready | None (I/O subsystem) | Non-preemptive | Hardware interrupt triggers transition |
| 8 | Ready ŌåÆ Running | Short-Term | Non-preemptive | STS picks P1 from Ready; P2 also in Ready but P1 was first |
| 12 | Running ŌåÆ Exit | None (Process action) | N/A | Process calls `exit()` |

**Key Insight**: In this trace, ALL transitions are **non-preemptive** because the system does not show any time-quantum based preemption (Running ŌåÆ Ready without voluntary action).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - **Five canonical states**: New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Exit (this is the KTU standard).
> - **Running state is unique** ŌĆö only ONE process per CPU core can be in Running at a time.
> - **Ready ŌēĀ Blocked**: Ready processes want the CPU; Blocked processes want an event.
> - **Three schedulers**: LTS (admission), STS (CPU selection), MTS (swapping).
> - **Suspended states** exist ONLY in systems with medium-term scheduling.
> - **PCB state field** is updated on EVERY transition ŌĆö this is the OS's source of truth.
> - **Preemptive** transition: Running ŌåÆ Ready forced by scheduler.
> - **Voluntary** transition: Running ŌåÆ Blocked initiated by process.
> - **State diagram must be labeled with triggers** for full marks in KTU exams.
> - **Formula to remember**: $F_{wasted} = \dfrac{T_{CS}}{q}$, so keep $q \gg T_{CS}$.
> - **Context switch** is not a state ŌĆö it is the MECHANISM enabling Ready Ōåö Running transitions.
> - **Turnaround Time** = Waiting Time + Burst Time (ignoring I/O for simplicity).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Thread?

A **thread** is the smallest unit of CPU execution that exists within a **process**. A traditional (heavyweight) process has a single thread of control, but a multithreaded process can have multiple threads ŌĆö each executing a different part of the program code, sharing the same code section, data section, and OS resources (open files, signals, etc.) while maintaining its own **thread ID**, **program counter (PC)**, **register set**, and **stack**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Silberschatz/Galvin/Gagne framework):**
> *A thread is a basic unit of CPU utilization; it comprises a thread ID, a program counter, a register set, and a stack. It shares with other threads belonging to the same process its code section, data section, and other operating-system resources such as open files and signals.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a large restaurant (the **process**). The restaurant has:
- One shared **kitchen** (code/data section)
- One shared **pantry** (heap, global variables)
- One shared **inventory** (open files, OS resources)

Now, a *traditional process* would be a restaurant with **one chef** doing everything ŌĆö chopping, cooking, plating, and serving. The chef can do only one task at a time. If the chef pauses to boil water, nothing else moves forward.

A **multithreaded process** is a restaurant with **multiple chefs** working in the same kitchen, sharing the same pantry and inventory. Each chef (thread) has:
- Their **own knife and cutting board** (register set + stack)
- Their **own recipe card** (program counter)
- A common understanding of the **menu** (code section)

One chef can chop vegetables while another boils pasta, and a third plates the dish ŌĆö all concurrently, all sharing the same resources. The kitchen itself is the process; the chefs are the threads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exams:** A process = **resource allocation unit**; a thread = **CPU execution / scheduling unit**. Multiple threads in one process = lighter, faster context switches than full process switches.

## 1.3 Motivation: Why Threads?

The two primary reasons for using threads are enumerated in the KTU syllabus:

1. **Responsiveness** ŌĆö In a single-threaded application, if one part of the program blocks (e.g., I/O wait), the entire program freezes. With multiple threads, one thread can continue executing while another waits.
2. **Resource Sharing** ŌĆö Threads share the memory and resources of the process by default, allowing automatic sharing without explicit inter-process communication (IPC) mechanisms.
3. **Economy** ŌĆö Creating, switching between, and terminating threads is much cheaper than performing the same operations on processes. (Memory allocation, kernel bookkeeping is minimized.)
4. **Scalability (Multiprocessor Utilization)** ŌĆö On multicore systems, threads can run in **true parallel** on different cores, achieving speedup that single-threaded code cannot.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love the **"Process vs. Thread"** comparison question. Memorize the table in Section 2.

## 1.4 The Single-Threaded vs. Multithreaded Process

| Component | Single-Threaded Process | Multithreaded Process |
|---|---|---|
| Registers | One set | One set **per thread** |
| Program Counter | One PC | One PC **per thread** |
| Stack | One stack | One stack **per thread** |
| Code Section | One (shared) | One (shared by all threads) |
| Data Section | One (shared) | One (shared by all threads) |
| OS Resources (Files, Sockets) | One set | One set (shared by all threads) |

This leads to the central principle: **Threads within a process share the process's resources; each thread has its own execution context.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process vs. Thread ŌĆö The Core Distinction

| Feature | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A program in execution; a unit of resource allocation | A unit of CPU scheduling within a process |
| Address Space | Each process has its **own** virtual address space | Threads **share** the address space of their parent process |
| Creation Cost | **Heavy** (memory allocation, PCB setup, kernel trap) | **Light** (often 10ŌĆō100├Ś cheaper) |
| Communication | IPC: pipes, sockets, shared memory, message queues | Direct via shared global memory (must synchronize) |
| Context Switch | **Expensive** (TLB flush, cache pollution, kernel mode) | **Cheaper** (mostly register + stack switch) |
| Termination | Frees all resources | Frees thread-local resources; process lives if one thread lives |
| Identifier | Process ID (PID) | Thread ID (TID) |
| Owned Resources | Open files, sockets, semaphores, memory, etc. | None of its own; borrows from process |

## 2.2 Types of Threads

### A. User-Level Threads (ULT)
- Thread management is done by a **user-level thread library** (e.g., `pthread` on older systems, GNU `pthreads`, `GNU Portable Threads`).
- The **kernel is unaware** of these threads ŌĆö to the kernel, the process is single-threaded.
- **Pros:** Fast (no kernel mode switch), customizable scheduling, runs on any OS.
- **Cons:** A blocking system call (e.g., `read()`) blocks the **entire process** (all threads). Cannot exploit multiprocessors natively.

### B. Kernel-Level Threads (KLT)
- Thread management is done by the **kernel** (Windows, Linux, modern Solaris).
- The kernel schedules each thread independently.
- **Pros:** True parallelism on multicore. Blocking call affects only one thread.
- **Cons:** Slower thread creation/switch (kernel mode transition).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Differentiate between ULT and KLT with examples."* The above table is your template.

## 2.3 Multithreading Models (The 3 Models)

These define how **user-level threads** are mapped to **kernel-level threads**.

### 2.3.1 Many-to-One Model
- Many ULTs ŌåÆ One KLT.
- Thread management done in user space.
- **Problem:** If one thread makes a blocking system call, the entire process blocks. No true parallelism on multiprocessors.
- **Examples:** Older Solaris Green Threads, GNU Portable Threads.

### 2.3.2 One-to-One Model
- Each ULT ŌåÆ One KLT.
- **Advantage:** True parallelism; one blocking thread does not block others.
- **Disadvantage:** Creating a user thread requires creating a corresponding kernel thread ŌåÆ overhead. The OS may limit the number of KLTs.
- **Examples:** Windows (Win32), Linux, modern Solaris.

### 2.3.3 Many-to-Many Model
- $m$ ULTs multiplexed onto $n$ KLTs, where typically $m \geq n$.
- Developers can create as many user threads as needed; the kernel can assign them to a smaller (or equal) pool of KLTs.
- Variations: **Two-level model** ŌĆö allows a bound thread (1:1) or multiplexed thread (m:n).
- **Examples:** Solaris before 9, Tru64 UNIX, HP-UX, IBM AIX.

## 2.4 Thread Libraries

A **thread library** provides the API for creating and managing threads.

| Library | Type | Platform | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pthreads (POSIX)** | Standardized API; can be ULT or KLT depending on implementation | Linux, macOS, UNIX | `pthread_create()`, `pthread_join()`, `pthread_exit()` |
| **Win32 Thread API** | Kernel-level (1:1) | Windows | `CreateThread()`, `WaitForSingleObject()` |
| **Java Threads** | Built on top of OS native threads (1:1 on modern JVMs) | Cross-platform (JVM) | `Thread` class, `Runnable` interface, `start()`, `run()` |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Performance & Multicore Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Threads have no "physics equations," but KTU loves the following performance formulas, especially under the **Multicore Programming** subsection of Module 3.

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | $S$ = speedup, $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = number of cores | Upper bound on speedup for $N$ cores |
| **Amdahl's Law (Alternative form)** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{1-f_s}{N}}$ | $f_s$ = serial fraction | Same law, $P = 1-f_s$ |
| **Maximum Speedup ($N \to \infty$)** | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$ | $P$ = parallel fraction | A program that is 95% parallel caps at speedup of $20\times$ |
| **CPU Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%$ | ŌĆö | KTU 2024 syllabus context: scheduling effectiveness |
| **Thread Creation Overhead Ratio (qualitative)** | $\text{Cost}_{\text{thread}} \ll \text{Cost}_{\text{process}}$ | ŌĆö | Often 10├ŚŌĆō100├Ś cheaper |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use the `|` character inside any table row.** In the Amdahl's law above, we used parentheses instead of absolute-value bars to remain markdown-table safe. The absolute-value form is $\vert x \vert$, but the table above is clean and unambiguous.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility ŌĆö Where Multithreading Matters in Practice

- **Web servers** (e.g., Apache with worker MPM, NGINX) use thread pools to handle thousands of simultaneous client requests.
- **Database engines** (PostgreSQL, MySQL) assign one thread per client connection.
- **GUI applications** keep the UI thread free of long computations; background threads perform heavy work and post results back to the UI.
- **Real-time systems and embedded OS** (RTEMS, VxWorks) rely on deterministic thread scheduling.
- **Compilers** (gcc, clang) parallelize build steps across cores ŌĆö directly relevant to **PERUT638 ŌĆō Compiler Design and Operating Systems**!
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Thread Lifecycle (State Diagram Steps)

A thread, like a process, moves through several states. The standard five-state model is:

$$\text{New} \rightarrow \text{Ready} \rightarrow \text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Blocked} \rightarrow \text{Terminated}$$

Below is a step-by-step trace of the **transitions**:

| Step | From State | To State | Trigger Event | Code-Level Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (none) | New | `pthread_create()` invoked | `pthread_create(&tid, NULL, fn, arg);` |
| 2 | New | Ready | OS admits thread to ready queue | Scheduler places TCB in queue |
| 3 | Ready | Running | Dispatcher selects the thread on a CPU | Context switch occurs |
| 4 | Running | Ready | Time quantum expires (preemptive) | Timer interrupt |
| 5 | Running | Blocked | Thread issues blocking I/O / waits on mutex | `pthread_mutex_lock(&m);` on a held lock |
| 6 | Blocked | Ready | I/O completes / mutex released | `read()` returns |
| 7 | Running | Terminated | Thread function returns / `pthread_exit()` | `return NULL;` |

## 3.2 Amdahl's Law ŌĆö Full Step-by-Step Derivation (Worked Example)

This is a guaranteed KTU problem. The setup: A program has a **parallel fraction $P = 0.90$** and a **serial fraction $1 - P = 0.10$**. We run it on $N = 4$ cores. What is the speedup?

### 3.2.1 Definition of Speedup

The **speedup** $S(N)$ is the ratio of the original (single-core) execution time to the new (multi-core) execution time:

$$S(N) = \frac{T(1)}{T(N)}$$

### 3.2.2 Decompose Execution Time

Let the total original work be $T(1) = 1$ (normalized). The parallel part takes time $P$ and the serial part takes time $(1-P)$. On $N$ cores, the parallel part can be split into $N$ sub-tasks running simultaneously, so it takes $\dfrac{P}{N}$ time. The serial part still takes $(1-P)$ time.

Therefore:

$$T(N) = (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}$$

### 3.2.3 Substitute

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

### 3.2.4 Plug in Numbers ($P = 0.90$, $N = 4$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(4) &= (1 - 0.90) + \frac{0.90}{4} \\
&= 0.10 + 0.225 \\
&= 0.325
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \frac{1}{0.325} \\
&\approx 3.076
\end{aligned}
$$

So a **90% parallel** program on **4 cores** achieves a speedup of approximately **3.08├Ś** (not 4├Ś).

### 3.2.5 Limit Case ŌĆö Infinite Cores

Take the limit as $N \to \infty$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} \\
&= \frac{1}{(1-P) + 0} \\
&= \frac{1}{1-P}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $P = 0.90$:

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.90} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Even with infinite cores, the program runs at most **10├Ś faster** because 10% of the work is serial. This is the **fundamental limit of parallelization** ŌĆö the foundation of Amdahl's Law.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Pthread Program Walkthrough

Consider the canonical "two threads print hello" example:

```c
#include <pthread.h>
#include <stdio.h>

void *worker(void *arg) {
    printf("Hello from thread, arg=%d\n", *(int *)arg);
    return NULL;
}

int main(void) {
    pthread_t t1, t2;
    int a = 10, b = 20;
    pthread_create(&t1, NULL, worker, &a);   // (i)   spawn thread 1
    pthread_create(&t2, NULL, worker, &b);   // (ii)  spawn thread 2
    pthread_join(t1, NULL);                  // (iii) wait thread 1
    pthread_join(t2, NULL);                  // (iv)  wait thread 2
    return 0;
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace (Analytical Matrix)

| Instruction | Thread that runs | Action | State transition |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Main | `pthread_create(&t1, ...)` | T1: New ŌåÆ Ready; main continues |
| (ii) | Main | `pthread_create(&t2, ...)` | T2: New ŌåÆ Ready; main continues |
| Scheduler picks T1 | T1 | `worker(&a)` starts | T1: Ready ŌåÆ Running |
| Scheduler picks T2 | T2 | `worker(&b)` starts | T2: Ready ŌåÆ Running |
| T1 prints & returns | T1 | `return NULL;` ŌåÆ `pthread_exit` | T1: Running ŌåÆ Terminated |
| T2 prints & returns | T2 | `return NULL;` ŌåÆ `pthread_exit` | T2: Running ŌåÆ Terminated |
| (iii) | Main | `pthread_join(t1, NULL)` | Main: Running ŌåÆ Blocked ŌåÆ Running (after T1 done) |
| (iv) | Main | `pthread_join(t2, NULL)` | Main: Running ŌåÆ Blocked ŌåÆ Running (after T2 done) |
| `return 0;` | Main | Process exit | All threads (process) ŌåÆ Terminated |

> [!TIP]
> Output order is **non-deterministic** ŌĆö the order in which "Hello from thread" lines appear depends on the scheduler. This is a classic KTU viva question.

## 3.4 Thread Cancellation ŌĆö Two Approaches

| Aspect | Asynchronous Cancellation | Deferred (Cooperative) Cancellation |
|---|---|---|
| When it occurs | Immediately, at an arbitrary point | At defined **cancellation points** (e.g., `pthread_testcancel()`) |
| Risk | Can leave shared resources in inconsistent state (no cleanup of mutexes, memory) | Safe; thread checks for cancellation at safe points |
| Default in Pthreads | Not supported by default; must explicitly request | **Default** type is `PTHREAD_CANCEL_DEFERRED` |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Signal Handling in Multithreaded Programs

| Method | Behavior | Used When |
|---|---|---|
| **Deliver signal to the thread to which it applies** | Per-thread signal mask determines reception | Synchronous signals (e.g., `SIGSEGV` from a fault) |
| **Deliver signal to every thread in the process** | Each thread runs its own signal handler | Global process-level signals (e.g., `SIGINT` from Ctrl+C) |
| **Deliver signal to certain threads only** | Application specifies recipient via `pthread_kill()` | Custom inter-thread signaling |
| **Designate a dedicated thread to receive all signals** | One thread `sigwait`s on the signal set | Server architectures |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 scheme explicitly lists **signal handling in multithreaded programs** as a topic. The matrix above is your high-yield answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Threaded vs. Multithreaded Process ŌĆö Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP["Single-Threaded Process"]
        SP_REG["Registers"]
        SP_PC["Program Counter"]
        SP_STACK["Stack"]
        SP_CODE["Code Section"]
        SP_DATA["Data Section"]
        SP_OS["OS Resources: Files, Sockets"]
    end
    SP_REG --- SP_PC
    SP_PC --- SP_STACK
    SP_STACK --- SP_CODE
    SP_CODE --- SP_DATA
    SP_DATA --- SP_OS

    subgraph MP["Multithreaded Process"]
        direction TB
        MP_CODE["Code Section SHARED"]
        MP_DATA["Data Section SHARED"]
        MP_OS["OS Resources SHARED"]

        subgraph T1["Thread 1"]
            T1_REG["Registers"]
            T1_PC["Program Counter"]
            T1_STACK["Stack"]
        end

        subgraph T2["Thread 2"]
            T2_REG["Registers"]
            T2_PC["Program Counter"]
            T2_STACK["Stack"]
        end

        subgraph T3["Thread 3"]
            T3_REG["Registers"]
            T3_PC["Program Counter"]
            T3_STACK["Stack"]
        end
    end

    T1 --- MP_CODE
    T2 --- MP_CODE
    T3 --- MP_CODE
    T1 --- MP_DATA
    T2 --- MP_DATA
    T3 --- MP_DATA
    T1 --- MP_OS
    T2 --- MP_OS
    T3 --- MP_OS
```

## 4.2 The Three Multithreading Models ŌĆö Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph U["User Space"]
        direction TB
        U1["ULT 1"]
        U2["ULT 2"]
        U3["ULT 3"]
        U4["ULT 4"]
    end

    subgraph K["Kernel Space"]
        K1["KLT 1"]
        K2["KLT 2"]
        K3["KLT 3"]
    end

    U1 -.-> K1
    U2 -.-> K1
    U3 -.-> K1
    U4 -.-> K1

    style U fill:#eef
    style K fill:#fee
```

> **Figure (a): Many-to-One Model** ŌĆö 4 ULTs multiplexed onto 1 KLT. The kernel sees only one execution flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph U2["User Space"]
        direction TB
        U1["ULT 1"]
        U2["ULT 2"]
        U3["ULT 3"]
    end

    subgraph K2["Kernel Space"]
        K1["KLT 1"]
        K2["KLT 2"]
        K3["KLT 3"]
    end

    U1 --> K1
    U2 --> K2
    U3 --> K3

    style U2 fill:#eef
    style K2 fill:#fee
```

> **Figure (b): One-to-One Model** ŌĆö Each ULT is bound to exactly one KLT. True parallelism possible.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph U3["User Space"]
        direction TB
        U1["ULT 1"]
        U2["ULT 2"]
        U3["ULT 3"]
        U4["ULT 4"]
        U5["ULT 5"]
    end

    subgraph K3["Kernel Space"]
        K1["KLT 1"]
        K2["KLT 2"]
    end

    U1 --> K1
    U2 --> K2
    U3 --> K1
    U4 --> K2
    U5 --> K1

    style U3 fill:#eef
    style K3 fill:#fee
```

> **Figure (c): Many-to-Many Model** ŌĆö 5 ULTs scheduled onto 2 KLTs by the thread library. The library may use LWP (lightweight processes) as intermediaries.

## 4.3 Thread Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New: pthread_create
    New --> Ready: Admitted by scheduler
    Ready --> Running: Dispatched on CPU
    Running --> Ready: Time quantum expires
    Running --> Blocked: I/O wait or mutex wait
    Blocked --> Ready: I/O complete or mutex released
    Running --> Terminated: Thread function returns
    Terminated --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: Implicit Threading with a Thread Pool

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Client submits task"] --> B{"Pool has idle thread?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Dispatch task to idle thread"]
    B -- No --> D{"Pool full?"}
    D -- No --> E["Create new worker thread"]
    D -- Yes --> F["Enqueue task in buffer"]
    F --> G["Wait for thread to free up"]
    G --> C
    E --> C
    C --> H["Worker executes task"]
    H --> I["Worker returns to pool: idle"]
```

> **Figure: Thread Pool Architecture** ŌĆö A common *implicit threading* technique where a fixed (or dynamic) set of worker threads services a queue of tasks. Used in web servers, GUI frameworks, and high-performance computing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a process and a thread.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Basic unit of | Resource allocation | CPU execution / scheduling |
| Address space | Each has its own | Shared with peer threads of same process |
| Context switch cost | High (TLB/cache flush, kernel mode) | Low (mostly register + stack switch) |
| Communication | Requires IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory) | Direct via shared globals (synchronization needed) |
| Creation overhead | Heavy (PCB allocation) | Light (TCB allocation, ~10ŌĆō100├Ś cheaper) |
| Identifier | PID (Process ID) | TID (Thread ID) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (1 mark lost):** Students often write "process is a program, thread is a part of a program." The KTU key expects the *resource-allocation vs. scheduling* distinction.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**What is meant by implicit threading? Mention any two techniques.**

**Model Answer:**
Implicit threading refers to the delegation of thread creation and management to **compilers, run-time libraries, or language runtimes**, rather than the application programmer explicitly creating threads.

Two common techniques:

1. **Thread Pools** ŌĆö A pre-created set of worker threads services a task queue; the application submits tasks to the pool.
2. **OpenMP** ŌĆö A compiler-directed API using `#pragma omp parallel` directives to parallelize loops in C/C++/Fortran.

*(Other valid answers: Grand Central Dispatch ŌĆö Apple platforms; Java Concurrency Framework `ExecutorService`.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer any **ONE** of the following. Each carries 14 marks (7 + 7).

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **three multithreading models** (many-to-one, one-to-one, many-to-many) with neat diagrams. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **many-to-many multithreading model using thread libraries**. Differentiate between **user-level threads (ULT)** and **kernel-level threads (KLT)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Q-A(a):

**Many-to-One Model:**
- Many user-level threads are mapped to a single kernel thread.
- Thread management is done in **user space** by a thread library.
- Only one thread can access the kernel at a time.
- **Examples:** Solaris Green Threads, GNU Portable Threads.

**One-to-One Model:**
- Each user thread is mapped to a separate kernel thread.
- Provides **more concurrency**; one thread's blocking system call does not block others.
- The drawback is that creating a user thread requires creating a corresponding kernel thread ŌĆö overhead.
- **Examples:** Windows NT/XP/2000, Linux, Solaris 9+.

**Many-to-Many Model:**
- Multiplexes $m$ user-level threads onto $n$ kernel-level threads (where $m \geq n$).
- The number of kernel threads may be specific to either a particular application or the machine.
- Developers can create as many user threads as necessary, while the kernel threads can run in parallel on a multiprocessor.
- **Variants include the two-level model**, where a user thread may be bound to a single kernel thread.

**Diagrams** ŌĆö Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the three models.

| Model | True Parallelism on Multiprocessor? | Blocking Syscall Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Many-to-One | No | Blocks entire process |
| One-to-One | Yes | Only blocks calling thread |
| Many-to-Many | Yes | Only blocks calling thread (via LWP) |

---

### Model Answer for Q-A(b):

**Thread Libraries ŌĆö Definition:**
A thread library provides the programmer with an API for creating and managing threads. There are two primary ways of implementing a thread library:

1. **Library entirely in user space** ŌĆö all code and data structures for the library exist in user space. Invoking a function in the library results in a local function call in user space, not a system call.
2. **Kernel-level library** ŌĆö code and data structures for the library exist in kernel space. Invoking a function in the library API results in a system call to the kernel.

**Three main thread libraries in use today:**
- **POSIX Pthreads** ŌĆö may be provided as either user-level or kernel-level.
- **Win32 threads** ŌĆö provided as a kernel-level library on Windows systems.
- **Java threads** ŌĆö typically implemented using the threads provided by the underlying OS (Pthreads on UNIX, Win32 on Windows).

**Pthreads** refers to the **POSIX standard** (IEEE 1003.1c) defining an API for thread creation and synchronization. This is the specification for thread behavior, not an implementation. The shared-memory, multithreaded programming model is:
- A program starts with one implicit thread (the main thread).
- Additional threads are created via `pthread_create()`.
- Each thread has its own stack and shared global data.
- Threads communicate via shared variables (must be synchronized).
- Thread terminates by calling `pthread_exit()` or returning from the thread function.

**Differentiating ULT and KLT** ŌĆö Refer to the table in Section 2.2 of these notes (type, management, parallelism, blocking behavior, examples).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**(a)** What are **threads**? Discuss the **benefits of multithreading** in an operating system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **threading issues** that arise in a multithreaded program. Explain **fork(), exec() system calls** in the context of multithreaded programs. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Q-B(a):

**Definition:**
A thread is a basic unit of CPU utilization. It comprises:
- A **thread ID**
- A **program counter**
- A **register set**
- A **stack space**

It shares with other threads belonging to the same process its **code section, data section, and other OS resources** (such as open files and signals). Traditionally, a process has a single thread of control. In a multithreaded system, a single process may have multiple threads, each capable of executing a different part of the code.

**Benefits of Multithreading:**

1. **Responsiveness** ŌĆö Multithreading an interactive application may allow a program to continue running even if part of it is blocked or is performing a lengthy operation, thereby increasing responsiveness to the user.
2. **Resource Sharing** ŌĆö By default, threads share the memory and other resources of the process; thus, multiple threads within a process can communicate with each other without invoking the kernel.
3. **Economy** ŌĆö Allocating memory and resources for process creation is costly. Because threads share the resources of the process to which they belong, it is more economical to create threads than to create a brand new process. Thread switching also has lower overhead than context switching.
4. **Scalability** ŌĆö A single-threaded process can run on only one CPU, no matter how many are available. Multithreading on a multi-CPU machine increases parallelism. (Amdahl's Law quantifies the limit.)

---

### Model Answer for Q-B(b):

**Threading Issues:**

1. **`fork()` and `exec()` system calls:**
   - If one thread in a program calls `fork()`, the **entire process** is duplicated ŌĆö including all threads (or sometimes only the calling thread, depending on the implementation).
   - If a thread in a multithreaded program calls `exec()`, the program image specified by the parameters of `exec()` replaces the entire process ŌĆö all threads except the calling thread are terminated.
   - On systems where `fork()` duplicates only the calling thread, any pending I/O from other threads in the original process is never processed because the new process is single-threaded.

2. **Signal Handling:**
   - Signals are used in UNIX systems to notify a process that a particular event has occurred.
   - In a single-threaded program, signal delivery is straightforward. In a multithreaded environment, however, the kernel must choose which thread to deliver the signal to.
   - Options: (a) deliver to the thread to which the signal applies, (b) deliver to every thread, (c) deliver to certain threads, (d) designate a specific thread to receive all signals.

3. **Thread Cancellation:**
   - A thread cancellation terminates a thread before it has completed. Two approaches:
     - **Asynchronous cancellation**: One thread immediately terminates the target thread.
     - **Deferred cancellation**: The target thread periodically checks whether it should terminate, allowing it an opportunity to clean up before exiting.

4. **Thread-Local Storage (TLS):**
   - Just as processes can have their own data, threads can have thread-specific data.
   - TLS allows each thread to have its own copy of certain data, useful for transactions and errno-style per-thread state.

5. **Scheduler Activations:**
   - A communication mechanism between the user-thread library and the kernel. The kernel provides a set of virtual processors (LWPs) to the application; the user-thread library schedules user threads onto LWPs. Upcalls notify the library of kernel events.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Threads Module:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse `fork()`'s behavior in multithreaded programs.** Many OS textbooks (and Linux behavior) differ ŌĆö clarify in your answer whether the implementation duplicates all threads or only the calling thread.
> 2. **Amdahl's Law:** If asked to compute speedup, **always normalize** $T(1) = 1$ and clearly state the **parallel fraction $P$** vs. the **serial fraction $(1-P)$**.
> 3. **Multithreading Models:** Don't just list them ŌĆö for each model, mention **true parallelism on multicore** and **blocking syscall behavior** (this is how KTU examiners differentiate between average and top answers).
> 4. **Pthread function arguments:** Be precise ŌĆö `pthread_create(&tid, NULL, fn, arg)` ŌĆö the *second* argument is the attribute object, not the argument to the thread function.
> 5. **TLS vs. Local Variables:** Local variables of a function are per-thread **only if the function is called by one thread at a time**; otherwise they are shared. TLS uses a key-based lookup (`pthread_key_create`).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Threads Module 3**

- **Definition:** Thread = `Thread ID + PC + Registers + Stack`, sharing process resources.
- **Why threads:** Responsiveness, Resource sharing, Economy, Scalability.
- **Process vs. Thread:** Process = resource owner; Thread = scheduled execution.
- **Two types:** ULT (user-managed) vs. KLT (kernel-managed).
- **Three multithreading models:**
  - **Many-to-One** ŌåÆ no parallel, blocks on syscall.
  - **One-to-One** ŌåÆ parallel, but creation overhead.
  - **Many-to-Many** ŌåÆ flexible, true parallelism.
- **Thread libraries:** Pthreads (POSIX), Win32, Java.
- **Thread states:** New ŌåÆ Ready ŌåÆ Running ŌåÆ Blocked ŌåÆ Terminated.
- **Threading issues:** `fork()`/`exec()`, signal handling, cancellation (async vs. deferred), TLS, scheduler activations.
- **Implicit threading:** Thread pools, OpenMP, `fork-join` parallelism.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$; max speedup at $N \to \infty$ is $\dfrac{1}{1-P}$.
- **Pthread essentials:** `pthread_create`, `pthread_join`, `pthread_exit`, `pthread_mutex_lock`, `pthread_cond_wait`.
- **Real-world uses:** Web servers, databases, GUI apps, compilers, embedded/real-time systems.

> **End of Module 3 ŌĆō Threads Notes (PERUT638)**
