**Step 2: Waiting Time via $WT_i = ST_i - AT_i$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 0 - 0 = 0 \\
WT_{P2} &= 10 - 0 = 10 \\
WT_{P3} &= 13 - 0 = 13 \\
WT_{P4} &= 18 - 0 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Average Waiting Time**

$$
\bar{WT} = \frac{0 + 10 + 13 + 18}{4} = \frac{41}{4} = 10.25 \text{ ms}
$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Compute Average Turnaround Time and identify the Convoy Effect. **[7 marks]**

**Step 1: TAT via $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 10 - 0 = 10 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 13 - 0 = 13 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 18 - 0 = 18 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 19 - 0 = 19
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Average TAT**

$$
\bar{TAT} = \frac{10 + 13 + 18 + 19}{4} = \frac{60}{4} = 15 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Convoy Effect Identification**

> [!NOTE]
> Yes, the **Convoy Effect** is present. P1 (BT=10) is the longest job and runs first, non-preemptively. P4 (BT=1) ŌĆö the shortest ŌĆö waits 18 ms. CPU utilization is 100%, but **device utilization and overall system throughput** are degraded. This is the classic symptom.

**Verification using alternate formula $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 10 - 10 = 0 \quad Ō£ō \\
WT_{P2} &= 13 - 3 = 10 \quad Ō£ō \\
WT_{P3} &= 18 - 5 = 13 \quad Ō£ō \\
WT_{P4} &= 19 - 1 = 18 \quad Ō£ō
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (Internal Choice) ŌĆö 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jul 2024]**

> With neat diagrams, explain the **two synchronization modes** of message passing in operating systems. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. Mention the role of the **kernel buffer** in each mode.

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Explain Blocking (Synchronous) and Non-Blocking (Asynchronous) primitives with diagrams. **[7 marks]**

**Blocking Send / Receive (Synchronous) ŌĆö 3.5 marks:**

The sender executes `send()` and **suspends** (blocks) until the message is delivered to and accepted by the receiver. Similarly, the receiver executes `receive()` and blocks until a message arrives.

```
Process P (Sender)                 Process Q (Receiver)
  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌŚÅŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ                    ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌŚÅŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ
       Ōöé                                 Ōöé
   send(P,Q,msg)                    receive(Q,msg)
       Ōöé                                 Ōöé
       Ō¢╝  (BLOCKED here)             (BLOCKED here)
       Ōöé                                 Ōöé
       ŌöéŌŚäŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ rendezvous / message handoff ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║
       Ōöé                                 Ōöé
       Ō¢╝  (resumes)                    (resumes)
  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌŚÅŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ                    ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌŚÅŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ
       Time passes ŌĆö both processes wait for each other
```

**Non-Blocking Send / Receive (Asynchronous) ŌĆö 3.5 marks:**

The sender hands the message to the kernel buffer and **continues immediately**; the receiver polls or uses a callback. Buffer must be finite (bounded) to prevent unbounded memory use.

```
Process P (Sender)                 Kernel Buffer
  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌŚÅŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ                       ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
       Ōöé                               Ōöé msg  Ōöé
   send(P,Q,msg)                       ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
       Ōöé  (returns immediately)            Ōöé
       Ō¢╝                                  Ō¢╝
  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌŚÅŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ  (continues)        Process Q reads later
                                      when scheduled
```

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Compare both modes and the role of the kernel buffer. **[7 marks]**

| Aspect | Blocking (Synchronous) | Non-Blocking (Asynchronous) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sender behavior** | Waits until received | Continues immediately |
| **Receiver behavior** | Waits until message arrives | Returns with flag or null |
| **Buffer need** | Minimal (or zero ŌĆö rendezvous) | Mandatory (bounded/unbounded) |
| **Speed** | Slower (process stalls) | Faster (no stall) |
| **Risk** | Deadlock (circular wait) | Buffer overflow, lost messages |
| **Use case** | Tightly-coupled rendezvous | Distributed/loosely-coupled |
| **Implementation** | Easier | Harder (needs buffer mgmt) |

**Role of Kernel Buffer (3 marks for elaboration):**
- Acts as the **intermediary** storage in the asynchronous case.
- Implemented as a bounded queue of message descriptors (`struct msg`).
- Capacity choices: **zero (rendezvous)**, **bounded (most common)**, **unbounded (theoretical)**.
- When full, sender either **blocks** (degrades to synchronous) or **errors out** (returns `-1` with `EAGAIN`).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Saying "non-blocking send never waits" ŌĆö this is wrong if the buffer is full. Many students lose 1 mark here. Always state the **bounded-buffer assumption** explicitly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in Module 3 questions:**
> 1. **Confusing WT formula** ŌĆö using $WT = CT - AT$ instead of $TAT - BT$. They are equal only for FCFS-by-arrival, NOT in general.
> 2. **Drawing Gantt charts without start-time labels** ŌĆö examiners *require* the `ST` and `CT` of every process.
> 3. **Forgetting the Convoy Effect explanation** in FCFS answers ŌĆö it is worth 1ŌĆō2 marks as a "comment".
> 4. **Mixing up direct vs. indirect** ŌĆö saying "Indirect = shared memory". Indirect is *still message passing*, just via a mailbox.
> 5. **Skipping the unit (ms)** in the final average ŌĆö always write "ms" or "time units" in numerical answers.
> 6. **In Message Passing, omitting the kernel** ŌĆö the kernel is the *active intermediary*; a diagram without it is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (FCFS + Message Passing):**
> - Message Passing = `send()` + `receive()` primitives, no shared memory, kernel-mediated.
> - Naming schemes: **Direct** (PŌåÆQ) vs. **Indirect** (via mailbox/port).
> - Synchronization: **Blocking (sync / rendezvous)** vs. **Non-blocking (async)**.
> - Buffer capacity: **Zero / Bounded / Unbounded**.
> - Scheduling Criteria (must memorize all 7): CPU Utilization, Throughput, TAT, WT, RT, Fairness, Predictability.
> - $TAT = CT - AT$ ; $WT = TAT - BT$ ; $WT = ST - AT$ (cross-check) ; $RT = \text{First CPU start} - AT$.
> - FCFS = **FIFO**, **non-preemptive**, simple, fair, starvation-free.
> - FCFS drawback = **Convoy Effect** (long head process blocks all tail processes).
> - FCFS is **not optimal** ŌĆö re-ordering same AT can lower $\bar{WT}$ (shown with $7.5 \to 3.75$ example).
> - In FCFS, since non-preemptive: $RT_i = WT_i$ for every process.
> - Always draw **Gantt chart** with start/end times ŌĆö partial credit in exams depends on it.
> - **Examiner's favorite line**: "Compare FCFS and SJF on the basis of average waiting time" ŌĆö be ready with a one-line contrast.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Round Robin Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Round Robin Scheduling ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Round Robin (RR) Scheduling** is a **preemptive, time-sharing** CPU scheduling algorithm designed specifically for **time-sharing systems** and **interactive environments**. It is one of the oldest, fairest, and most widely used scheduling algorithms, especially in mainframe and modern operating systems. In RR scheduling, the OS maintains a **ready queue** of processes (typically a circular FIFO queue). Each process is assigned a fixed time unit called the **Time Quantum (TQ)** or **Time Slice**. The CPU scheduler dispatches the process at the head of the ready queue, runs it for at most one time quantum, and then: 1. If the process completes within the quantum, it is terminated and released from the system. 2. If the process has not completed, it is **preempted** by the timer interrupt and appended to the **tail (rear) of the ready queue**. 3. The CPU scheduler then dispatches the next process from the head of the ready queue. This cyclic re-queuing mechanism gives every runnable process an equal, periodic share of the CPU ŌĆö hence the name "Round Robin" (a French term meaning "round ribbon," historically used in a circular signed petition). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 ŌĆö CPU Scheduling):** > Round Robin is a **preemptive algorithm** based on the **FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)** policy augmented with **clock interrupts** and a **fixed time quantum**. It explicitly favors *response time* over throughput, making it ideal for interactive systems. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Pizza Slice" Intuition Imagine **8 hungry friends** sitting around a circular table sharing **one large pizza**. No one is allowed to take a bite whenever they want ŌĆö that would be chaotic. Instead, you enforce a rule: - A **timer (the round-robin referee)** gives each friend exactly **30 seconds** to eat. - The moment the buzzer rings, that friend **must put the slice down**, even if they are still hungry. - The pizza then moves to the next friend in clockwise order. - If a friend finishes their 30 seconds early because they are full, they are **removed from the table**. - The cycle continues until everyone is done. **Translation to OS terms:** - The **pizza** = the CPU. - The **8 friends** = processes in the ready queue. - The **30-second rule** = the **Time Quantum (TQ)**. - The **buzzer** = the **hardware timer interrupt** that triggers preemption. - The **clockwise rotation** = the **circular ready queue**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why use a circular queue?** > Because every process must get a "turn" periodically. Just as a circular queue has no real "end," every process keeps coming back to the head ŌĆö guaranteeing **starvation freedom** and **fair CPU allocation**. ### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary) | Term | Symbol | Definition | |---|---|---| | Time Quantum | $T_q$ | The fixed time slice allocated to each process per CPU burst. | | Arrival Time | $A_i$ | The instant at which process $P_i$ enters the ready queue. | | Burst Time | $B_i$ | Total CPU time required by process $P_i$. | | Completion Time | $C_i$ | The instant at which $P_i$ finishes execution. | | Turnaround Time | $TAT_i$ | $C_i - A_i$ | | Waiting Time | $WT_i$ | $TAT_i - B_i$ | | Response Time | $RT_i$ | Time from arrival to first CPU allocation. | | Context Switch Overhead | $CS$ | Time lost when swapping processes. | > [!TIP] > In **KTU 2024 Scheme** numerical problems, you will always be given $A_i$ and $B_i$ (or only $B_i$ for simple cases where all processes arrive at $t=0$). The examiner expects you to draw a **Gantt chart** first, then compute $TAT$ and $WT$ ŌĆö in that exact order. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Mechanics ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic The Round Robin scheduler is governed by a small, elegant set of rules. Let us enumerate them precisely: 1. **Initialization:** Place all processes that have arrived at time $0$ into the **ready queue** in order of their arrival (or process ID if simultaneous). 2. **Dispatch:** Dequeue the process at the **head** of the ready queue and assign it the CPU. 3. **Execution Limit:** Run the process for **at most** $T_q$ time units. The hardware timer is programmed to fire an interrupt at the end of $T_q$. 4. **Decision Point (Preemption Check):** When the timer fires: - **If** the process's remaining burst time $\le T_q$, it completes; record its **Completion Time** and deallocate the PCB. - **Else** the process is **preempted**: its state is saved, and it is **enqueued at the tail** of the ready queue. 5. **Arrivals Mid-Quantum:** If a new process arrives while the CPU is busy, it is added to the **tail** of the ready queue (or at the appropriate position to maintain arrival order). It will not be scheduled until all processes already in the queue ahead of it have had a turn. 6. **Termination:** When the ready queue becomes empty, the CPU becomes idle (or dispatches the system idle process). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** A common source of lost marks is misplacing newly arriving processes. The correct convention is: > - If a new process $P_{new}$ arrives at time $t_{arr}$ **while another process is executing**, $P_{new}$ is **added to the tail of the ready queue immediately at $t_{arr}$**. > - It will be picked up *after* the currently running process's quantum ends and *after* all processes already in the queue. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Turnaround Time | $TAT_i = C_i - A_i$ | time units | Time from arrival to completion. | | Waiting Time | $WT_i = TAT_i - B_i$ | time units | Time spent in the ready queue. | | Response Time | $RT_i = \text{first CPU allocation} - A_i$ | time units | First response; $\ge 0$. | | Average TAT | $\overline{TAT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i$ | time units | Higher = worse turnaround. | | Average WT | $\overline{WT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i$ | time units | Lower = better scheduling. | | CPU Utilization | $U = 1 - \dfrac{\text{total idle time}}{\text{total time}}$ | fraction | Aim for $U \to 1$. | | Throughput | $\Theta = \dfrac{n}{\text{Total Time}}$ | proc/unit time | Higher = better. | | Context Switch Count | $N_{cs} = \left\lceil \dfrac{B_i}{T_q} \right\rceil$ (summed) | integer | For one process. | > [!NOTE] > **Note on absolute value notation in tables:** Since the markdown table syntax uses `|` as a column separator, we represent absolute value using `\vert` or `\mid` (LaTeX) and avoid raw `|` symbols inside cells. For example, $|x|$ is written as $\vert x \vert$ in KTU notes. ### 2.3 The Critical Role of the Time Quantum The **Time Quantum ($T_q$)** is the single most important tunable parameter in RR scheduling. It dictates the algorithm's entire personality: - **If $T_q \to \infty$:** RR degenerates into **FCFS** (no preemption ever triggers). - **If $T_q \to 0$:** The system spends *all* its time on **context switching** and *zero* time on useful work ŌĆö this is called **"convoy effect" inverted to its worst** (also called **processor sharing** in the limit). - **If $T_q$ is very large** (e.g., $\ge \max B_i$): Equivalent to non-preemptive FCFS. - **If $T_q$ is very small** (e.g., $\ll$ context switch time): Throughput collapses; CPU utilization drops drastically. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rule of Thumb (commonly tested):** A "good" time quantum lies in the range > $$ 10 \text{ ms} \;\le\; T_q \;\le\; 100 \text{ ms} $$ > A widely cited heuristic (from Silberschatz et al., *Operating System Concepts*) is: > $$ T_q \;\approx\; 80\%\; \text{of CPU bursts should be shorter than } T_q $$ > In other words, $T_q$ should be **slightly longer than the typical CPU burst** so that most processes finish in one quantum. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Round Robin is not just a textbook algorithm ŌĆö it is the **de facto scheduler** of: - **Linux CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler):** Uses a *weighted* round-robin variant (vruntime-based red-black tree). - **Windows NT kernel:** Employs a multi-level feedback queue that uses RR for interactive threads. - **Real-time systems:** Modified RR (Rate Monotonic, for example) is used in hard real-time scheduling. - **Network packet routers:** Round-robin packet scheduling (DRR ŌĆö Deficit Round Robin) is industry standard. - **Cloud load balancers:** Web server request dispatch often uses weighted RR. The algorithm's popularity stems from its **starvation-freedom**, **simplicity** ($O(1)$ dispatch), and **predictable response time**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked-Out KTU Numerical ŌĆö Building a Gantt Chart **Problem (Model Question, 14-Mark KTU Pattern):** Consider the following set of processes, each with an associated arrival time and CPU burst time. Compute the **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** using **Round Robin Scheduling** with **Time Quantum = 4 units**. | Process | Arrival Time ($A_i$) | Burst Time ($B_i$) | |---|---|---| | $P_1$ | 0 | 8 | | $P_2$ | 1 | 4 | | $P_3$ | 2 | 9 | | $P_4$ | 3 | 5 | ### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Gantt Chart Construction > [!NOTE] > **Convention used:** The **ready queue** is a FIFO. When a new process arrives during execution, it is **appended to the tail** at the moment of arrival. The currently running process consumes the entire remaining part of its quantum (or finishes, whichever is earlier). **Initial State at $t = 0$:** - Ready Queue: $[P_1]$ - Running: none - **Decision:** Dispatch $P_1$. Timer set for $\min(8, 4) = 4$ units. **Step 1: $t = 0 \to t = 4$** - $P_1$ runs for 4 units. Remaining burst of $P_1 = 8 - 4 = 4$. - During this interval, $P_2$ arrives at $t=1$, $P_3$ at $t=2$, $P_4$ at $t=3$. - At $t=4$, the timer fires. $P_1$ has not finished (4 units remain). - **Action:** Preempt $P_1$, enqueue it at the tail. - Ready Queue at $t=4$: $[P_2, P_3, P_4, P_1]$ - **Decision:** Dispatch $P_2$. **Step 2: $t = 4 \to t = 8$** - $P_2$ has burst $B_2 = 4$, so it needs exactly $4$ units and **finishes** at $t=8$. - $P_2$ is **removed** from the system. $C_2 = 8$. - Ready Queue at $t=8$: $[P_3, P_4, P_1]$ - **Decision:** Dispatch $P_3$. **Step 3: $t = 8 \to t = 12$** - $P_3$ runs for 4 units. Remaining burst of $P_3 = 9 - 4 = 5$. - No new arrivals (all four have already arrived by $t=3$). - At $t=12$, timer fires. $P_3$ has not finished (5 units remain). - **Action:** Preempt $P_3$, enqueue at tail. - Ready Queue at $t=12$: $[P_4, P_1, P_3]$ - **Decision:** Dispatch $P_4$. **Step 4: $t = 12 \to t = 16$** - $P_4$ has burst $B_4 = 5$, runs for 4 units. Remaining burst of $P_4 = 5 - 4 = 1$. - At $t=16$, timer fires. $P_4$ has not finished (1 unit remains). - **Action:** Preempt $P_4$, enqueue at tail. - Ready Queue at $t=16$: $[P_1, P_3, P_4]$ - **Decision:** Dispatch $P_1$. **Step 5: $t = 16 \to t = 20$** - $P_1$ has remaining burst $4$, runs for 4 units, **finishes** at $t=20$. - $C_1 = 20$. $P_1$ removed. - Ready Queue at $t=20$: $[P_3, P_4]$ - **Decision:** Dispatch $P_3$. **Step 6: $t = 20 \to t = 24$** - $P_3$ has remaining burst $5$, runs for 4 units. Remaining $= 5 - 4 = 1$. - At $t=24$, timer fires. $P_3$ has not finished (1 unit remains). - **Action:** Preempt $P_3$, enqueue at tail. - Ready Queue at $t=24$: $[P_4, P_3]$ - **Decision:** Dispatch $P_4$. **Step 7: $t = 24 \to t = 25$** - $P_4$ has remaining burst $1$, runs for 1 unit and **finishes** at $t=25$. - $C_4 = 25$. $P_4$ removed. - Ready Queue at $t=25$: $[P_3]$ - **Decision:** Dispatch $P_3$. **Step 8: $t = 25 \to t = 26$** - $P_3$ has remaining burst $1$, runs for 1 unit and **finishes** at $t=26$. - $C_3 = 26$. $P_3$ removed. - Ready Queue at $t=26$: $[\,]$ (empty). **CPU becomes idle.** ### 3.3 Final Gantt Chart $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Gantt Chart:} \quad & \vert P_1 \vert P_2 \vert P_3 \vert P_4 \vert P_1 \vert P_3 \vert P_4 \vert P_3 \vert \\ & 0 \;\; 4 \;\;\; 8 \;\;\; 12 \;\;\; 16 \;\;\; 20 \;\;\; 24 \;\;\; 25 \;\;\; 26 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Performance Metric Computation Using the formulas from the cheat sheet: $$ \begin{aligned} TAT_1 &= C_1 - A_1 = 20 - 0 = 20 \\ TAT_2 &= C_2 - A_2 = 8 - 1 = 7 \\ TAT_3 &= C_3 - A_3 = 26 - 2 = 24 \\ TAT_4 &= C_4 - A_4 = 25 - 3 = 22 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} WT_1 &= TAT_1 - B_1 = 20 - 8 = 12 \\ WT_2 &= TAT_2 - B_2 = 7 - 4 = 3 \\ WT_3 &= TAT_3 - B_3 = 24 - 9 = 15 \\ WT_4 &= TAT_4 - B_4 = 22 - 5 = 17 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{20 + 7 + 24 + 22}{4} = \frac{73}{4} = 18.25 \text{ units} \\[4pt] \overline{WT} &= \frac{12 + 3 + 15 + 17}{4} = \frac{47}{4} = 11.75 \text{ units} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.5 Analytical Summary Table (Result Matrix) | Process | $A_i$ | $B_i$ | $C_i$ | $TAT_i$ | $WT_i$ | # of Quanta Used | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $P_1$ | 0 | 8 | 20 | 20 | 12 | 2 (4 + 4) | | $P_2$ | 1 | 4 | 8 | 7 | 3 | 1 (4) | | $P_3$ | 2 | 9 | 26 | 24 | 15 | 3 (4 + 4 + 1) | | $P_4$ | 3 | 5 | 25 | 22 | 17 | 2 (4 + 1) | | **Total** | ŌĆö | **26** | ŌĆö | **73** | **47** | **8 quanta** | | **Average** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **18.25** | **11.75** | ŌĆö | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification check:** $\sum B_i = 26$ must equal $C_{\max} = 26$. Ō£ō **Total CPU work = 26**, **CPU idle time = 0**, hence **CPU Utilization = 100%**. This is the standard KTU verification step. ### 3.6 Effect of Varying Time Quantum ŌĆö Analytical Comparison Let us now analyze the **same process set** under different time quanta to build intuition (a frequent KTU 2-mark question). | $T_q$ | $\overline{WT}$ | Behavior | Equivalence | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 19.0 | Excessive preemption; many context switches | Approaches *processor sharing* | | 2 | 14.5 | Heavy preemption; high overhead | Pure RR | | 4 | 11.75 | **Balanced (our case)** | Sweet spot | | 9 | 9.5 | Less preemption | Approaches FCFS | | $\infty$ (FCFS) | 7.75 | No preemption | FCFS | > [!NOTE] > This monotonic decrease in $\overline{WT}$ as $T_q \to \infty$ is a *general property only when all processes arrive at $t=0$*. With staggered arrivals, the picture is more complex ŌĆö a favorite KTU trick question. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of the RR Scheduler ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IN[" INPUT LAYER "] A0["Process P1 with Arrival A1 and Burst B1"] A1["Process P2 with Arrival A2 and Burst B2"] A2["Process P3 with Arrival A3 and Burst B3"] A3["Process Pn with Arrival An and Burst Bn"] end subgraph CORE[" ROUND ROBIN SCHEDULING CORE "] Q0["New Process Arrives"] Q1{"Process Already Running?"} Q2["Add to TAIL of Ready Queue"] Q3["CPU Dispatcher Picks HEAD of Queue"] Q4["Set Hardware Timer to Time Quantum Tq"] Q5["Process Px Executes on CPU"] Q6{"Timer Interrupt Fired?"} Q7{"Process Completed?"} Q8["Enqueue at TAIL of Ready Queue"] Q9["Remove PCB and Release Resources"] Q10{"Ready Queue Empty?"} Q11["CPU Idle / System Idle Process"] end subgraph OUT[" OUTPUT LAYER "] Z0["Gantt Chart"] Z1["Completion Times Ci"] Z2["Turnaround Times TATi"] Z3["Waiting Times WTi"] Z4["Average Metrics"] end A0 --> Q0 A1 --> Q0 A2 --> Q0 A3 --> Q0 Q0 --> Q1 Q1 -- Yes --> Q2 Q1 -- No --> Q3 Q2 --> Q3 Q3 --> Q4 Q4 --> Q5 Q5 --> Q6 Q6 -- No --> Q5 Q6 -- Yes --> Q7 Q7 -- Yes --> Q9 Q7 -- No --> Q8 Q8 --> Q3 Q9 --> Z0 Q9 --> Z1 Z1 --> Z2 Z2 --> Z3 Z3 --> Z4 Q9 --> Q10 Q10 -- No --> Q3 Q10 -- Yes --> Q11 ``` ### 4.2 State Transition Diagram for a Process in RR ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> New New --> Ready : Admitted by Long-Term Scheduler Ready --> Running : Dispatched by CPU Scheduler Running --> Ready : Timer Interrupt after Time Quantum Tq Running --> Terminated : Burst Complete Ready --> Terminated : Killed by User or System Running --> Waiting : I/O or Event Request Waiting --> Ready : I/O Complete or Event Occurs Terminated --> [*] note right of Running : A timer interrupt ALWAYS sends the process back to Ready after exactly Tq units, unless it terminates first. ``` ### 4.3 Gantt Chart Timeline Visualization (for the Worked Example) ```mermaid gantt title Round Robin Gantt Chart (Time Quantum = 4) dateFormat X axisFormat %s section CPU Timeline P1 Execution :a1, 0, 4 P2 Execution :a2, 4, 4 P3 Execution :a3, 8, 4 P4 Execution :a4, 12, 4 P1 Execution 2 :a5, 16, 4 P3 Execution 2 :a6, 20, 4 P4 Execution 2 :a7, 24, 1 P3 Execution 3 :a8, 25, 1 ``` ### 4.4 Ready Queue Evolution (Round-by-Round Trace) ```mermaid flowchart LR R0["t=0
Queue: P1
Dispatch: P1"] --> R1 R1["t=4
Queue: P2, P3, P4, P1
Dispatch: P2"] --> R2 R2["t=8
Queue: P3, P4, P1
P2 done, Dispatch: P3"] --> R3 R3["t=12
Queue: P4, P1, P3
Dispatch: P4"] --> R4 R4["t=16
Queue: P1, P3, P4
Dispatch: P1"] --> R5 R5["t=20
Queue: P3, P4
P1 done, Dispatch: P3"] --> R6 R6["t=24
Queue: P4, P3
Dispatch: P4"] --> R7 R7["t=25
Queue: P3
P4 done, Dispatch: P3"] --> R8 R8["t=26
Queue: empty
P3 done, CPU IDLE"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The **circular** nature of the ready queue is conceptually clear from this trace: every preempted process is appended to the tail, ensuring it eventually returns to the head. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model 1]** *Define Round Robin scheduling. Why is it classified as a preemptive algorithm? What is the role of the time quantum?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Round Robin (RR) scheduling** is a **preemptive CPU scheduling algorithm** designed for time-sharing systems, in which each process in the ready queue is assigned a **fixed time unit called the Time Quantum ($T_q$)**. The CPU scheduler dispatches processes in a **circular FIFO order**, and when the timer interrupt fires at the end of the quantum, the currently running process is **preempted** and placed at the **tail of the ready queue**, while the next process is dispatched. > > It is **preemptive** because the running process can be **forcibly suspended by a hardware timer interrupt** before it voluntarily relinquishes the CPU ŌĆö a hallmark of preemption. > > The **time quantum** determines the **length of CPU allocation per process per turn**; it directly governs the trade-off between **response time** (small $T_q$ ŌåÆ better response) and **throughput / context switch overhead** (large $T_q$ ŌåÆ better throughput). A well-chosen $T_q$ balances both. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model 2]** *What happens when the time quantum in Round Robin scheduling is made too small or too large? Explain with a suitable justification.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > - **If $T_q$ is too small** (e.g., $T_q \ll$ context switch time): The CPU spends the majority of its cycles performing **context switches** rather than executing user processes. Throughput collapses, **CPU utilization drops**, and average turnaround time degrades. In the limit $T_q \to 0$, the system approaches **processor sharing** but with zero useful work. > - **If $T_q$ is too large** (e.g., $T_q \ge \max B_i$): The RR algorithm degenerates into **FCFS (non-preemptive)**, as no process ever gets preempted mid-burst. This hurts **response time** for short jobs and short interactive processes. > - **Optimal $T_q$**: Should be **greater than the typical CPU burst** so that most processes finish in one quantum, yet **small enough to give quick response** to interactive users. A widely cited range is **10 ms to 100 ms**. ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice**. You attempt **one** of two alternatives. Each alternative has sub-parts (typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks). Always **draw the Gantt chart first** before computing metrics. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** Consider four processes $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ with the following arrival and burst times. Apply **Round Robin Scheduling with Time Quantum = 3** and draw the Gantt chart. **(7 Marks)** | Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time | |---|---|---| | $P_1$ | 0 | 6 | | $P_2$ | 1 | 3 | | $P_3$ | 2 | 8 | | $P_4$ | 3 | 4 | **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Initial Queue at $t=0$:** $[P_1]$. Dispatch $P_1$. **Step 2: $t = 0 \to t = 3$** - $P_1$ runs 3 units; remaining $= 6 - 3 = 3$. - Arrivals: $P_2$ at $t=1$, $P_3$ at $t=2$. - Timer fires at $t=3$. $P_4$ arrives at $t=3$ and is **added to tail immediately**. - Queue at $t=3$: $[P_2, P_3, P_4, P_1]$. Dispatch $P_2$. **Step 3: $t = 3 \to t = 6$** - $P_2$ has burst $3$, runs exactly 3 units, **finishes** at $t=6$. $C_2 = 6$. - Queue at $t=6$: $[P_3, P_4, P_1]$. Dispatch $P_3$. **Step 4: $t = 6 \to t = 9$** - $P_3$ runs 3 units; remaining $= 8 - 3 = 5$. - No new arrivals. - Timer fires at $t=9$. Queue: $[P_4, P_1, P_3]$. Dispatch $P_4$. **Step 5: $t = 9 \to t = 12$** - $P_4$ has burst $4$, runs 3 units; remaining $= 4 - 3 = 1$. - Timer fires at $t=12$. Queue: $[P_1, P_3, P_4]$. Dispatch $P_1$. **Step 6: $t = 12 \to t = 15$** - $P_1$ has remaining $3$, runs 3 units, **finishes** at $t=15$. $C_1 = 15$. - Queue at $t=15$: $[P_3, P_4]$. Dispatch $P_3$. **Step 7: $t = 15 \to t = 18$** - $P_3$ runs 3 units; remaining $= 5 - 3 = 2$. - Timer fires at $t=18$. Queue: $[P_4, P_3]$. Dispatch $P_4$. **Step 8: $t = 18 \to t = 19$** - $P_4$ has remaining $1$, runs 1 unit, **finishes** at $t=19$. $C_4 = 19$. - Queue at $t=19$: $[P_3]$. Dispatch $P_3$. **Step 9: $t = 19 \to t = 21$** - $P_3$ has remaining $2$, runs 2 units, **finishes** at $t=21$. $C_3 = 21$. **Final Gantt Chart:** $$ \begin{aligned} & \vert P_1 \vert P_2 \vert P_3 \vert P_4 \vert P_1 \vert P_3 \vert P_4 \vert P_3 \vert \\ & 0 \quad\; 3 \quad\; 6 \quad\; 9 \quad\; 12 \quad\; 15 \quad\; 18 \quad\; 19 \quad\; 21 \end{aligned} $$ **Performance Metrics:** $$ \begin{aligned} TAT_1 &= 15 - 0 = 15, \quad &WT_1 &= 15 - 6 = 9 \\ TAT_2 &= 6 - 1 = 5, \quad &WT_2 &= 5 - 3 = 2 \\ TAT_3 &= 21 - 2 = 19, \quad &WT_3 &= 19 - 8 = 11 \\ TAT_4 &= 19 - 3 = 16, \quad &WT_4 &= 16 - 4 = 12 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \overline{TAT} = \frac{15+5+19+16}{4} = \frac{55}{4} = 13.75 $$ $$ \overline{WT} = \frac{9+2+11+12}{4} = \frac{34}{4} = 8.5 $$ **(b)** Compare **Round Robin** scheduling with **FCFS** and **SJF (non-preemptive)** in terms of (i) preemptiveness, (ii) average waiting time, (iii) convoy effect, and (iv) suitability for time-sharing systems. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer (Tabular Comparison):** | Criterion | FCFS | SJF (Non-Preemptive) | Round Robin | |---|---|---|---| | **(i) Preemptiveness** | Non-preemptive | Non-preemptive | **Preemptive** (timer-based) | | **(ii) Average Waiting Time** | High (convoy effect) | **Optimal** (provably minimum for non-preemptive) | Moderate; better than FCFS, worse than SJF | | **(iii) Convoy Effect** | **Severe** (one slow process blocks all) | Moderate | **None** (no process monopolizes CPU) | | **(iv) Time-Sharing Suitability** | Poor (no quick response) | Poor (no quick response) | **Excellent** (fast, predictable response) | | Starvation Possible? | No | **Yes** (long jobs may starve) | **No** (every process gets periodic turns) | | Overhead | Very low | Low | **Higher** (context switches per quantum) | | Knowledge of Burst Time? | Not required | **Required** (often unknown in practice) | Not required | > **Conclusion:** Round Robin is the **preferred choice for time-sharing and interactive systems** because it delivers **fair, fast, and starvation-free CPU allocation** at the cost of higher context-switch overhead. SJF gives better average waiting time but is rarely usable in practice (burst time unknown) and can starve long jobs. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the role of the **Time Quantum** in Round Robin scheduling. What are the consequences of choosing a **very small** or **very large** time quantum? Illustrate with a Gantt chart for the process set below using $T_q = 2$. **(7 Marks)** | Process | Arrival | Burst | |---|---|---| | $P_1$ | 0 | 4 | | $P_2$ | 0 | 3 | | $P_3$ | 0 | 2 | **Model Solution:** **Role of Time Quantum:** - $T_q$ is the **maximum continuous CPU time** any process can hold before being preempted. - It governs the **trade-off between response time and throughput**. **Consequences:** - **Very small $T_q$:** Many preemptions ŌåÆ poor throughput, high overhead, low CPU utilization. - **Very large $T_q$:** Behaves like FCFS ŌåÆ poor response time for interactive jobs. **Gantt Chart with $T_q = 2$:** **Step 1: $t=0$:** Queue $[P_1, P_2, P_3]$. Dispatch $P_1$. **Step 2: $t=0 \to t=2$:** $P_1$ runs 2 units; remaining $= 4 - 2 = 2$. Preempt. - Queue: $[P_2, P_3, P_1]$. Dispatch $P_2$. **Step 3: $t=2 \to t=4$:** $P_2$ runs 2 units; remaining $= 3 - 2 = 1$. Preempt. - Queue: $[P_3, P_1, P_2]$. Dispatch $P_3$. **Step 4: $t=4 \to t=6$:** $P_3$ runs 2 units; **finishes** at $t=6$. $C_3 = 6$. - Queue: $[P_1, P_2]$. Dispatch $P_1$. **Step 5: $t=6 \to t=8$:** $P_1$ has remaining $2$, runs 2 units, **finishes** at $t=8$. $C_1 = 8$. - Queue: $[P_2]$. Dispatch $P_2$. **Step 6: $t=8 \to t=9$:** $P_2$ has remaining $1$, runs 1 unit, **finishes** at $t=9$. $C_2 = 9$. **Final Gantt Chart:** $$ \begin{aligned} & \vert P_1 \vert P_2 \vert P_3 \vert P_1 \vert P_2 \vert \\ & 0 \quad\; 2 \quad\; 4 \quad\; 6 \quad\; 8 \quad\; 9 \end{aligned} $$ **Performance Metrics:** $$ \begin{aligned} TAT_1 &= 8 - 0 = 8, \quad &WT_1 &= 8 - 4 = 4 \\ TAT_2 &= 9 - 0 = 9, \quad &WT_2 &= 9 - 3 = 6 \\ TAT_3 &= 6 - 0 = 6, \quad &WT_3 &= 6 - 2 = 4 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \overline{TAT} = \frac{8+9+6}{3} = \frac{23}{3} \approx 7.67 $$ $$ \overline{WT} = \frac{4+6+4}{3} = \frac{14}{3} \approx 4.67 $$ **(b)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of Round Robin scheduling. Why is it considered **starvation-free** and **fair**? **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Advantages:** 1. **Starvation-free:** Every process in the ready queue is guaranteed CPU access within a bounded time. 2. **Fair allocation:** Each process gets an equal share of CPU time per cycle. 3. **Excellent response time:** Short and interactive jobs get quick first response. 4. **No convoy effect:** Unlike FCFS, slow processes do not block fast ones. 5. **Simple implementation:** Just a circular FIFO queue; $O(1)$ dispatch. 6. **No knowledge of burst time required:** Unlike SJF, RR works with any burst distribution. **Disadvantages:** 1. **Higher context-switch overhead** if $T_q$ is small. 2. **Lower throughput** than FCFS for CPU-bound workloads. 3. **Average waiting time can be high** for long processes if many short processes are present. 4. **Performance is highly sensitive to time quantum choice** ŌĆö poor choice can cause either starvation of response or wasted CPU. 5. **Not optimal** in terms of average turnaround time (SJF is provably better in that metric). **Why starvation-free and fair:** - The **circular queue** ensures that every process, once enqueued, is **guaranteed to be dispatched** in finite time. - The **bounded quantum** prevents any single process from monopolizing the CPU. - Hence, no process can be indefinitely denied the CPU ŌĆö making RR both **starvation-free** and **inherently fair**. > [!WARNING] > **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to enqueue newly arriving processes immediately:** A new process arriving at $t=2$ must be added to the tail of the ready queue *at* $t=2$, not at the next quantum boundary. (Penalty: 1ŌĆō2 marks per occurrence.) > 2. **Confusing burst time with remaining burst time:** After the first quantum, you must track *remaining* burst, not original burst. Drawing the Gantt chart on a fresh piece of paper is the safest way to avoid this. > 3. **Not verifying the final answer:** Always check that $\sum B_i = C_{\max}$. This catches 90% of arithmetic errors. > 4. **Mixing up TAT and WT formulas:** $TAT_i = C_i - A_i$ and $WT_i = TAT_i - B_i$ ŌĆö write them in this order; do not compute $WT$ first. > 5. **Forgetting to state that RR is preemptive:** Many students describe RR but omit the word "preemptive" or "timer interrupt" ŌĆö both are mandatory keywords for full marks. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **­¤ÜĆ Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Round Robin Scheduling** - Ō£ģ RR is a **preemptive, time-sharing** algorithm based on **circular FCFS** with **timer-driven preemption**. - Ō£ģ The **Time Quantum ($T_q$)** is the maximum continuous CPU time per dispatch. - Ō£ģ Preempted processes go to the **tail** of the ready queue; newly arriving processes are added to the tail **at the moment of arrival**. - Ō£ģ **Starvation-free** and **fair** by construction (circular queue guarantees bounded waiting). - Ō£ģ **Optimal $T_q$** Ōēł 10ŌĆō100 ms; should be **slightly greater** than the typical CPU burst. - Ō£ģ **Formulas to memorize (verbatim for KTU):** - $TAT_i = C_i - A_i$ - $WT_i = TAT_i - B_i$ - $\overline{TAT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum TAT_i$, $\overline{WT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum WT_i$ - Ō£ģ **Limits:** - $T_q \to \infty$ ŌåÆ FCFS behavior. - $T_q \to 0$ ŌåÆ all context switches, zero useful work (processor sharing limit). - Ō£ģ **Disadvantages:** Higher overhead due to context switches; not optimal for throughput. - Ō£ģ **Verification rule:** $\sum B_i$ must equal the last completion time $C_{\max}$. - Ō£ģ **Real-world users:** Linux CFS (weighted RR), Windows thread scheduler, network routers (DRR), cloud load balancers. - Ō£ģ **Comparison anchor:** RR has **better response time** than FCFS/SJF but **worse average waiting time** than SJF. > **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question says "compute the Gantt chart, TAT, and WT using Round Robin," always ŌĆö **always** ŌĆö start by listing the **ready queue state at $t=0$**, then proceed *one quantum at a time*, updating the queue at every preemption point. This disciplined, step-by-step approach is what differentiates a 14/14 answer from a 9/14 answer.

topics.title: Race Conditions - Critical Section Problem - Peterson's Solution - Semaphores - Synchronization Problems - Producer Consumer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Race Conditions - Critical Section Problem - Peterson's Solution - Semaphores - Synchronization Problems - Producer Consumer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Race Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Race Condition (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A **race condition** is a situation in a concurrent/multiprogrammed system where the final outcome of a process execution depends on the **order or relative timing** in which multiple processes access and manipulate shared resources (shared variables, shared memory, shared files).

### Intuitive Analogy: The Shared Bathroom Mirror

Imagine two roommates, **Ram** and **Krishna**, sharing a single bathroom mirror and a single tube of toothpaste at 8:00 AM. If both grab the tube at *exactly* the same instant:

- Ram presses the tube to take some paste.
- Krishna simultaneously presses the tube to take some paste.

The "final amount of paste squeezed out" depends entirely on whose hand movement finished a millisecond first. The behavior of the system is **non-deterministic** ŌĆö sometimes Ram wins, sometimes Krishna wins, and sometimes they both fail to get paste. This is exactly what a race condition is in an Operating System: two or more processes access a shared resource concurrently, and the result is unpredictable.

### Formal Causes of a Race Condition

1. **Shared Mutable State**: Two or more processes share a variable, buffer, or device.
2. **Interleaved Execution**: The OS scheduler may preempt one process in the middle of an operation and schedule another.
3. **Non-Atomic Operations**: Operations like `counter = counter + 1` are actually three CPU instructions (LOAD, ADD, STORE), and preemption can split them.

### Atomicity ŌĆö The Cure

> [!NOTE]
> An **atomic operation** is one that appears to the rest of the system as if it were executed **instantaneously as a single, indivisible unit**, without any interleaving from other processes.

---

## 1.2 The Critical Section Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Section (KTU Definition):**
> A **Critical Section** is a segment of code in a process that accesses shared resources (shared variables, shared data structures, shared devices) and must be executed in a **mutually exclusive** manner ŌĆö only one process can be inside its critical section at any given time.

### Structure of a Typical Process

A general process $P_i$ can be decomposed into four logical regions:

| Region | Purpose | Access to Shared Data? |
|---|---|---|
| **Entry Section** | Requests permission to enter the CS | Yes (sets flags) |
| **Critical Section** | Actual code manipulating shared data | **Yes ŌĆö exclusive access required** |
| **Exit Section** | Releases permission, cleans up flags | Yes (resets flags) |
| **Remainder Section** | Everything else (computation, I/O prep) | No |

### Three Formal Requirements for a Valid Critical Section Solution

For any solution to the critical section problem, KTU examiners expect these **three mandatory conditions** (often asked for 3 marks):

1. **Mutual Exclusion (ME)**: If process $P_i$ is executing in its critical section, then no other process $P_j$ ($j \neq i$) can be executing in its critical section at the same time.
2. **Progress (P)**: If no process is currently in the critical section and there are some processes that wish to enter it, then only those processes that are *not* in their remainder section should participate in deciding which process will enter the critical section next. This decision cannot be postponed indefinitely.
3. **Bounded Waiting (BW)**: There must exist a bound (a finite limit) on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter their critical sections after a process has made a request to enter its critical section and before that request is granted. This prevents **starvation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bonus (4th condition ŌĆö sometimes asked):** **No assumption about relative CPU speeds** and **no assumption about the number of CPUs** in the system.

---

## 1.3 Peterson's Solution ŌĆö Brief Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peterson's Solution (KTU Definition):**
> Peterson's solution is a **classic software-only synchronization algorithm** proposed by *Gary L. Peterson in 1981* for solving the critical section problem for **two processes**. It uses only shared variables (no hardware atomic instructions) and guarantees **Mutual Exclusion, Progress, and Bounded Waiting**.

It is the most widely tested synchronization algorithm in KTU examinations because it neatly combines two ideas: a **"turn" variable** (who goes next) and a **"flag" array** (intent declaration).

---

## 1.4 Semaphores ŌĆö Brief Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semaphore (KTU Definition ŌĆö Dijkstra, 1965):**
> A **semaphore** is a synchronization tool (an integer variable) that is accessed **only through two atomic, indivisible operations**: `wait()` (also called `P()` or `down()`) and `signal()` (also called `V()` or `up()`). It is used to control access to shared resources in concurrent systems.

- **Binary Semaphore**: Value is either **0 or 1** (acts like a lock). Used for **mutual exclusion**.
- **Counting Semaphore**: Value can be any **non-negative integer**. Used for **resource counting** (e.g., N identical buffers).

---

## 1.5 Producer-Consumer (Bounded Buffer) Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Producer-Consumer Problem (KTU Definition):**
> The Producer-Consumer problem (also called the **Bounded Buffer Problem**) is a classic synchronization problem where a **producer process** generates data items and places them into a shared buffer of fixed size $N$, while a **consumer process** removes these items. The system must ensure that:
> - The producer does not put items into a **full** buffer.
> - The consumer does not remove items from an **empty** buffer.
> - The buffer is accessed in a **mutually exclusive** manner.

This is the **flagship synchronization problem** in KTU Module 4, almost guaranteed to appear for full 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Race Condition ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

Consider two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ both executing `counter = counter + 1`, where `counter` is a shared variable initially equal to **5**.

The single line `counter = counter + 1` decomposes into **three machine instructions**:

| Step | Instruction (by $P_1$) | Register State | Memory `counter` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LOAD counter into R1 | R1 = 5 | 5 |
| 2 | ADD R1, 1 | R1 = 6 | 5 |
| 3 | STORE R1 into counter | R1 = 6 | 6 |

Now suppose the OS scheduler interleaves $P_2$ between steps 2 and 3:

| Step | Process | Action | R1($P_1$) | R2($P_2$) | counter |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_1$ | LOAD | 5 | ŌĆö | 5 |
| 2 | $P_1$ | ADD | 6 | ŌĆö | 5 |
| **3** | **$P_2$** | **LOAD** | 6 | **5** | 5 |
| **4** | **$P_2$** | **ADD** | 6 | **6** | 5 |
| **5** | **$P_2$** | **STORE** | 6 | 6 | **6** |
| 6 | $P_1$ | STORE | 6 | 6 | **6** ŌØī |

> [!WARNING]
> **Expected value after 2 increments = 7, but actual value = 6.** One increment was silently lost. This is a race condition bug.

---

## 2.2 Critical Section Requirements ŌĆö Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Mutual Exclusion

If process $P_i$ is in CS $\Rightarrow$ no $P_j$ ($j \neq i$) is in CS.

Mathematical formulation: At any instant $t$, let $n(t)$ be the number of processes in CS.

$$\forall t, \quad n(t) \leq 1$$

### 2.2.2 Progress

If no process is in CS and some process *wants* to enter CS, then *eventually* one of them must be allowed in. The decision cannot be made by processes that are in their Remainder Section.

### 2.2.3 Bounded Waiting

If $P_i$ has expressed interest at time $t_0$, then there exists a finite $K$ such that $P_i$ enters the CS within at most $K$ entries of other processes.

$$\text{Wait time of } P_i \leq K \times T_{CS}$$

where $T_{CS}$ is the average time spent in the critical section.

---

## 2.3 Peterson's Solution ŌĆö Operational Logic

Peterson's solution uses **two shared variables** for two processes $P_0$ and $P_1$:

| Variable | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `flag[2]` | Boolean array, initially `false` | `flag[i] = true` means process $i$ **wants** to enter CS |
| `turn` | Integer, initially 0 or 1 | Whose turn it is if both want to enter |

### Algorithm for Process $P_i$ (where the other process is $P_j$, and $j = 1 - i$)

```
do {
    flag[i] = true;        // "I want to enter"
    turn = j;              // "But it's your turn first"
    while (flag[j] && turn == j)  // Wait if other wants in AND it's their turn
        ; /* busy wait */
    
    // ----- CRITICAL SECTION -----
    
    flag[i] = false;       // "I'm done"
    
    // ----- REMAINDER SECTION -----
} while (true);
```

### Why Both Variables?

- `flag[i] = true` alone causes **race on flag** (both set to true simultaneously ŌåÆ both enter).
- `turn` alone causes **strict alternation** (starvation).
- Combined: If both `flag[i]` and `flag[j]` are true, the last one to write `turn` will yield to the other. This elegantly resolves who enters first.

---

## 2.4 Semaphores ŌĆö The High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **board-exam essential**. Memorize all entries.

| Concept | Symbol / Notation | Initial Value | Atomic Operations | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Binary Semaphore** | $S \in \{0, 1\}$ | `S = 1` | `wait(S)` / `signal(S)` | Mutual Exclusion |
| **Counting Semaphore** | $S \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ | `S = N` (resource count) | `wait(S)` / `signal(S)` | Resource Pool, Producer-Consumer |
| **P / wait** | `wait(S)` | ŌĆö | `while (S <= 0) ; S--;` | Decrement / block if 0 |
| **V / signal** | `signal(S)` | ŌĆö | `S++;` | Increment / wakeup waiter |
| **Mutex** | `mutex` | `mutex = 1` | `wait(mutex); CS; signal(mutex);` | Locks for shared data |
| **Empty slots** | `empty` | `empty = N` | `wait(empty);` | Counts free buffer slots |
| **Full slots** | `full` | `full = 0` | `wait(full);` | Counts filled buffer slots |

### Atomic Definitions of wait() and signal()

```
wait(S) {                          // Also called P(S) or down(S)
    while (S <= 0)                 // spinlock / busy-wait
        ;                          // (or block process ŌĆö kernel)
    S = S - 1;
}

signal(S) {                        // Also called V(S) or up(S)
    S = S + 1;
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both `wait()` and `signal()` must be executed **atomically**. In modern kernels, they are implemented using hardware-supported atomic instructions (e.g., `test-and-set`, `compare-and-swap`) or by disabling interrupts. This is why semaphores overcome the limitations of pure software solutions like Peterson's.

### Two Types of Semaphores (Important Distinction)

| Type | Mechanism | Effect on Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Spinlock / Busy-Wait Semaphore** | Continuously loops, consuming CPU | Wastes CPU cycles but low overhead |
| **Blocking Semaphore (Kernel-level)** | Process is moved to a waiting queue; another runs | No CPU wastage; context-switch overhead |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Databases**: Row-level locking uses counting semaphores.
- **RTOS (VxWorks, FreeRTOS)**: Binary semaphores for task synchronization.
- **Embedded Multithreading**: Producer-Consumer drives sensor data pipelines.
- **Linux Kernel**: Uses `sem_t` (POSIX) and `struct semaphore` (kernel-internal).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Consolidated)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Number of buffer slots | Problem-specific (e.g., $N = 5$) |
| `empty` | Number of empty slots | `N` (initially) |
| `full` | Number of occupied slots | `0` (initially) |
| `mutex` | Binary lock | `1` (initially) |
| $T_{CS}$ | Avg. time in critical section | ŌĆö |
| $K$ | Bounded waiting bound | Finite integer |
| `flag[i]` | Intent of process $i$ | `false` (initially) |
| `turn` | Whose turn it is | 0 or 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: A common mistake is initializing `empty = 0` and `full = N`. The correct initialization is `empty = N` (all slots empty) and `full = 0` (no items yet).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Analytical Proof: Peterson's Solution Satisfies Mutual Exclusion

We will rigorously prove that **Peterson's solution guarantees Mutual Exclusion** between two processes $P_0$ and $P_1$.

### Assumption for Contradiction

Assume that **both** $P_0$ and $P_1$ are in their critical sections simultaneously at some time $t$. We will derive a contradiction.

### Step 1: Both Processes Must Have Set Their Flags

For $P_0$ to be in its CS, it must have exited the `while` loop:

$$\text{flag[0]} = \text{true} \quad \text{(set just before the while loop)}$$

Similarly for $P_1$:

$$\text{flag[1]} = \text{true}$$

### Step 2: The `while` Loop Condition Must Have Been False

For $P_0$ to exit the busy-wait, the loop condition must evaluate to false:

$$(\text{flag[1]} = \text{false}) \quad \text{OR} \quad (\text{turn} \neq 1)$$

But we already established `flag[1] = true`, so the second clause must hold:

$$\text{turn} \neq 1 \implies \text{turn} = 0$$

### Step 3: Symmetric Argument for $P_1$

For $P_1$ to exit its busy-wait:

$$(\text{flag[0]} = \text{false}) \quad \text{OR} \quad (\text{turn} \neq 0)$$

Since `flag[0] = true`, the second clause must hold:

$$\text{turn} \neq 0 \implies \text{turn} = 1$$

### Step 4: The Contradiction

From Step 2: $\text{turn} = 0$.

From Step 3: $\text{turn} = 1$.

Therefore:

$$\text{turn} = 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{turn} = 1$$

This is a logical contradiction. Our assumption that both processes are in the critical section simultaneously is **FALSE**.

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{Peterson's Solution guarantees Mutual Exclusion.} \quad \blacksquare}$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Analytical Proof: Progress & Bounded Waiting in Peterson's Solution

### Progress

Consider a state where neither process is in the CS, and $P_0$ wants to enter:

- $P_0$ sets `flag[0] = true` and `turn = 1`.
- If $P_1$ does not want to enter, then `flag[1] = false`.
- The while condition for $P_0$ becomes: `flag[1] && turn == 1` $\equiv$ `false && ...` $\equiv$ **false**.
- $P_0$ enters immediately. Progress is preserved.

### Bounded Waiting

Suppose $P_0$ is waiting while $P_1$ enters and exits the CS $K$ times. Each time $P_1$ exits, it sets `flag[1] = false`. If $P_0$ still wishes to enter, it sets `flag[0] = true` (already true). When $P_1$ re-enters, it executes `turn = j = 0`, **yielding the turn to $P_0$**. Thus $P_0$ enters within at most **one** extra entry of $P_1$.

$$\text{Bounded Waiting Constant } K = 1 \quad \text{(excellent bound!)}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Semaphore Implementation of Mutual Exclusion

For $n$ processes accessing a shared resource, define a binary semaphore `mutex` with initial value `1`:

$$\text{mutex} = 1$$

### Process Structure

```
Process Pi {
    while (true) {
        // ----- Remainder Section -----
        wait(mutex);          // Entry section
        // ----- Critical Section -----
        signal(mutex);        // Exit section
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace (Two Processes)

| Step | Action | `mutex` value | Process in CS? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial state | **1** | None |
| 1 | $P_1$ calls `wait(mutex)` | **0** | $P_1$ |
| 2 | $P_2$ calls `wait(mutex)` | 0 | $P_2$ blocks (mutex = 0) |
| 3 | $P_1$ calls `signal(mutex)` | **1** | $P_2$ unblocks |
| 4 | $P_2$ (now scheduled) calls `wait(mutex)` | **0** | $P_2$ |
| 5 | $P_2$ calls `signal(mutex)` | **1** | None |

> [!NOTE]
> Mutual Exclusion is enforced because only one process can decrement `mutex` from 1 to 0. The second process is blocked until the first signals.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Producer-Consumer Solution Using Semaphores

### Setup

Let the buffer size be $N$. We initialize **three semaphores**:

$$\text{empty} = N, \quad \text{full} = 0, \quad \text{mutex} = 1$$

> **Roles:**
> - `empty` counts the number of **empty slots** available.
> - `full` counts the number of **occupied slots** available.
> - `mutex` provides **mutual exclusion** on the buffer itself.

### Producer Code

```
Producer {
    while (true) {
        // 1. Produce an item
        item = produce();
        
        wait(empty);          // 2. Wait if buffer is full
        wait(mutex);          // 3. Acquire lock on buffer
        
        // 4. CRITICAL SECTION: Add item to buffer
        buffer[in] = item;
        in = (in + 1) % N;
        
        signal(mutex);        // 5. Release lock
        signal(full);         // 6. Increment count of full slots
    }
}
```

### Consumer Code

```
Consumer {
    while (true) {
        wait(full);           // 1. Wait if buffer is empty
        wait(mutex);          // 2. Acquire lock on buffer
        
        // 3. CRITICAL SECTION: Remove item from buffer
        item = buffer[out];
        out = (out + 1) % N;
        
        signal(mutex);        // 4. Release lock
        signal(empty);        // 5. Increment count of empty slots
        
        // 6. Consume the item
        consume(item);
    }
}
```

### Analytical Trace (Buffer size $N = 3$, two producers, one consumer)

Assume `empty = 3`, `full = 0`, `mutex = 1`, `in = 0`, `out = 0`.

| Step | Process | Action | `empty` | `full` | `mutex` | Buffer state |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Init | ŌĆö | **3** | **0** | **1** | `[ , , ]` |
| 1 | Producer A | `wait(empty)` | 2 | 0 | 1 | empty |
| 2 | Producer A | `wait(mutex)` | 2 | 0 | 0 | empty |
| 3 | Producer A | Insert at `in=0` | 2 | 0 | 0 | `[A, , ]` |
| 4 | Producer A | `signal(mutex)` | 2 | 0 | 1 | `[A, , ]` |
| 5 | Producer A | `signal(full)` | 2 | **1** | 1 | `[A, , ]` |
| 6 | Producer B | `wait(empty)` | **1** | 1 | 1 | `[A, , ]` |
| 7 | Producer B | `wait(mutex)` | 1 | 1 | 0 | `[A, , ]` |
| 8 | Producer B | Insert at `in=1` | 1 | 1 | 0 | `[A,B, ]` |
| 9 | Producer B | `signal(mutex)` | 1 | 1 | 1 | `[A,B, ]` |
| 10 | Producer B | `signal(full)` | 1 | **2** | 1 | `[A,B, ]` |
| 11 | Consumer | `wait(full)` | 1 | **1** | 1 | `[A,B, ]` |
| 12 | Consumer | `wait(mutex)` | 1 | 1 | 0 | `[A,B, ]` |
| 13 | Consumer | Remove at `out=0` | 1 | 1 | 0 | `[ ,B, ]` |
| 14 | Consumer | `signal(mutex)` | 1 | 1 | 1 | `[ ,B, ]` |
| 15 | Consumer | `signal(empty)` | **2** | 1 | 1 | `[ ,B, ]` |

### Correctness Conditions Verified

1. **Producer blocks on full buffer**: When `empty = 0`, `wait(empty)` causes producer to block.
2. **Consumer blocks on empty buffer**: When `full = 0`, `wait(full)` causes consumer to block.
3. **No race on buffer**: Only one process inside `wait(mutex)`...`signal(mutex)` region at a time.
4. **No deadlock**: `wait(empty)` and `wait(mutex)` order is **carefully chosen** ŌĆö taking resource-count semaphore (`empty`/`full`) BEFORE `mutex` prevents a classic deadlock.

> [!WARNING]
> **DEADLOCK WARNING (KTU Frequent Pitfall):**
> If the order is swapped to `wait(mutex)` first then `wait(empty)`, deadlock is possible:
> - Producer A acquires `mutex`, sees buffer full, blocks on `wait(empty)`.
> - Consumer acquires `mutex`? ŌĆö **NO**, `mutex` is held by Producer A. But consumer blocks on `wait(full)` ... actually producer A is stuck, consumer blocks on `wait(full)` if buffer is full. ŌåÆ **Deadlock** if producer holds `mutex` and waits on `empty`.
> 
> Therefore the standard KTU-accepted order is: **`wait(empty)` then `wait(mutex)` for Producer**, and **`wait(full)` then `wait(mutex)` for Consumer**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Solutions

| Property | Peterson's Solution | Semaphore (Mutex) | Hardware (TSL) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mutual Exclusion** | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes |
| **Progress** | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes (blocking) | Ō£ģ Yes |
| **Bounded Waiting** | Ō£ģ Yes ($K=1$) | Ō£ģ Yes (FIFO queue) | Depends |
| **Number of processes** | Only 2 | Any $n$ | Any $n$ |
| **Requires busy-waiting** | Yes (spinlock) | No (blocking) | Yes |
| **Atomic hardware needed** | No (pure software) | Yes (atomic increment) | Yes (TSL) |
| **Used in modern OS** | No (academic only) | Ō£ģ Yes (Linux `sem_t`) | Ō£ģ Yes (spinlocks) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Structure of a Process with Critical Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Process Pi] --> B[Remainder Section]
    B --> C[Entry Section<br/>Request permission]
    C --> D{Can I enter CS?}
    D -- No --> E[Busy Wait / Block]
    E --> D
    D -- Yes --> F[Critical Section<br/>Access shared data]
    F --> G[Exit Section<br/>Release permission]
    G --> B
```

## 4.2 Peterson's Solution ŌĆö Logical Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P0 as Process P0
    participant SHARED as Shared Variables
    participant P1 as Process P1
    Note over SHARED: flag[0]=false, flag[1]=false, turn=0
    P0->>SHARED: flag[0] = true
    P0->>SHARED: turn = 1
    P1->>SHARED: flag[1] = true
    P1->>SHARED: turn = 0
    Note over P0,P1: Both processes now check the while loop
    P0->>SHARED: while flag[1] AND turn==1 ?
    SHARED-->>P0: turn==0, so condition is FALSE
    P0->>P0: Enter Critical Section
    P1->>SHARED: while flag[0] AND turn==0 ?
    SHARED-->>P1: condition is TRUE, wait
    P1->>P1: Busy wait
    P0->>SHARED: flag[0] = false
    P0->>P0: Exit Critical Section
    P1->>SHARED: condition now FALSE
    P1->>P1: Enter Critical Section
```

## 4.3 Semaphore wait() and signal() Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process calls wait S] --> B{S greater than 0?}
    B -- Yes --> C[S = S minus 1]
    C --> D[Enter Critical Section]
    B -- No --> E[Block process<br/>Add to waiting queue]
    E --> F[Wait until another process signals S]
    F --> G{Another process called signal S?}
    G -- Yes --> H[S = S plus 1]
    H --> I[Wake up a blocked process]
    I --> J[Process moves to ready queue]
```

## 4.4 Producer-Consumer Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Producer
        P1[Produce Item]
        P2[wait empty]
        P3[wait mutex]
        P4[Insert into buffer]
        P5[signal mutex]
        P6[signal full]
        P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6
    end
    P6 --> Buffer[(Shared Buffer<br/>Size N)]
    Buffer --> C1
    subgraph Consumer
        C1[wait full]
        C2[wait mutex]
        C3[Remove from buffer]
        C4[signal mutex]
        C5[signal empty]
        C6[Consume Item]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6
    end
```

## 4.5 Buffer as Circular Queue (Index Movement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Buffer_N3[Buffer of size 3]
        S0[Slot 0]
        S1[Slot 1]
        S2[Slot 2]
        S0 --> S1 --> S2 --> S0
    end
    IN[Index in] --> S0
    OUT[Index out] --> S1
    Note1[After each insert<br/>in = in + 1 mod 3]
    Note2[After each remove<br/>out = out + 1 mod 3]
```

## 4.6 State Transition Diagram for Semaphore Value

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S1: Initial value 1
    S1 --> S0: Process calls wait
    S0 --> Blocked: wait called again, process blocks
    S0 --> S1: Process calls signal
    Blocked --> S0: signal called, one process unblocks
    Blocked --> Ready: Process dequeued to ready queue
    Ready --> S0: Rescheduled and acquires semaphore
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "race condition". Explain how it leads to inconsistent results with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **race condition** occurs when multiple processes access and manipulate shared data concurrently, and the final result depends on the **order of execution/interleaving** of these processes.

**Example (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for example):**
Consider two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ executing `counter = counter + 1` concurrently, with `counter = 5` initially. The single statement compiles to three machine instructions: `LOAD`, `ADD`, `STORE`.

If $P_1$ loads `counter = 5`, but before it stores the incremented value `6`, $P_2$ also loads `counter = 5` and stores `6`, then both processes store `6`. The expected value after 2 increments is **7**, but the actual stored value is **6**. One increment is **lost** due to non-atomic interleaved access.

> **Marking Scheme:**
> - 1 mark: Correct definition.
> - 1 mark: Listing the three machine instructions.
> - 1 mark: Demonstrating the lost-update scenario with final values.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the three requirements that a valid solution to the critical section problem must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ŌĆö 1 mark each):**

1. **Mutual Exclusion**: If process $P_i$ is executing in its critical section, no other process $P_j$ can simultaneously be in its critical section.

2. **Progress**: If no process is currently in the critical section and there exist processes that wish to enter, only those processes not in their remainder section participate in selecting the next entrant. This selection cannot be postponed indefinitely.

3. **Bounded Waiting**: There must be a bound on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter their critical sections after a process has requested entry and before its request is granted. This guarantees no **starvation**.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE allows Module Internal Choice. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain Peterson's Solution to the critical section problem for two processes. Show that it satisfies mutual exclusion and bounded waiting.**

**Model Solution:**

#### Peterson's Algorithm

For two processes $P_0$ and $P_1$, two shared variables are used:

- `int turn` ŌĆö whose turn it is to enter (initially 0).
- `boolean flag[2]` ŌĆö `flag[i]` indicates that $P_i$ is ready to enter.

Code for Process $P_i$ (where $j = 1 - i$):

```
do {
    flag[i] = true;       // Step 1: Declare interest
    turn = j;             // Step 2: Yield to other process
    while (flag[j] && turn == j)   // Step 3: Wait
        ;                 // busy wait
    // ----- CRITICAL SECTION -----
    flag[i] = false;      // Step 4: Exit indicator
    // ----- REMAINDER SECTION -----
} while (true);
```

#### Proof of Mutual Exclusion [3 marks]

Assume for contradiction that **both** $P_0$ and $P_1$ are in CS simultaneously.

1. For $P_0$ to be in CS, it must have exited its `while` loop. Since `flag[1] = true` (otherwise $P_1$ wouldn't have entered CS), the only way $P_0$'s loop terminates is:

$$\text{turn} \neq 1 \implies \text{turn} = 0$$

2. Similarly for $P_1$ to be in CS, since `flag[0] = true`:

$$\text{turn} \neq 0 \implies \text{turn} = 1$$

3. Both cannot hold simultaneously ŌåÆ **Contradiction**. Hence ME is guaranteed.

#### Proof of Bounded Waiting [2 marks]

If $P_0$ is waiting and $P_1$ enters the CS, then $P_1$ sets `flag[1] = false` on exit. If $P_0$ still wants to enter, when $P_1$ next wishes to enter, it executes `turn = j = 0`, yielding the turn to $P_0$. So $P_0$ enters within **at most 1** extra entry by $P_1$.

$$\text{Bounded waiting constant } K = 1$$

#### Limitations [2 marks]

- Works only for **two processes**.
- Uses **busy waiting** (wastes CPU).
- Not guaranteed on modern **out-of-order execution** CPUs without memory barriers.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Define a Semaphore. Explain the wait() and signal() operations. Distinguish between binary and counting semaphores.**

**Model Solution:**

#### Definition [2 marks]

A **semaphore** is a synchronization tool (introduced by *Edsger W. Dijkstra in 1965*) that is an integer variable accessed only through two atomic operations: `wait()` (P operation) and `signal()` (V operation).

#### wait() and signal() [3 marks]

```
wait(S) {                  // P(S) / down(S)
    while (S <= 0)
        ;                  // busy wait OR block process
    S = S - 1;
}

signal(S) {                // V(S) / up(S)
    S = S + 1;
}
```

Both must be executed **atomically**.

#### Binary vs Counting Semaphore [2 marks]

| Feature | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Value range | 0 or 1 | Any non-negative integer |
| Initial value | 1 | N (number of resources) |
| Use case | Mutual Exclusion | Resource counting |
| Example | `mutex = 1` | `empty = N`, `full = 0` |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the Producer-Consumer problem. Write the solution using semaphores. Explain how the solution avoids deadlock.**

**Model Solution:**

#### Problem Description [2 marks]

The Producer-Consumer problem (also called Bounded Buffer) involves two types of processes:
- A **producer** generates data items and puts them into a shared buffer of fixed size $N$.
- A **consumer** removes data items from the buffer.

Constraints:
- Producer must wait if buffer is **full**.
- Consumer must wait if buffer is **empty**.
- Buffer access must be **mutually exclusive**.

#### Semaphore Solution [4 marks]

Three semaphores are used:

- `empty = N` ŌĆö counts empty slots.
- `full = 0` ŌĆö counts filled slots.
- `mutex = 1` ŌĆö for mutual exclusion.

**Producer code:**

```
Producer() {
    while (true) {
        item = produce();
        wait(empty);
        wait(mutex);
        buffer[in] = item;
        in = (in + 1) % N;
        signal(mutex);
        signal(full);
    }
}
```

**Consumer code:**

```
Consumer() {
    while (true) {
        wait(full);
        wait(mutex);
        item = buffer[out];
        out = (out + 1) % N;
        signal(mutex);
        signal(empty);
        consume(item);
    }
}
```

#### Deadlock Avoidance [1 mark]

The order of `wait()` calls is critical:
- Producer calls `wait(empty)` **before** `wait(mutex)`.
- Consumer calls `wait(full)` **before** `wait(mutex)`.

This ensures that a process **never holds `mutex` while waiting for a resource-count semaphore**, preventing circular wait. Therefore **no deadlock** occurs.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain Peterson's Solution with its structure. State any two limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

#### Structure [5 marks]

Peterson's solution uses two shared variables:
- `flag[2]` (boolean array, initially false).
- `turn` (integer, 0 or 1).

For process $P_i$ with the other process $P_j$:

```
do {
    flag[i] = TRUE;
    turn = j;
    while (flag[j] && turn == j);
    
    CRITICAL SECTION
    
    flag[i] = FALSE;
    
    REMAINDER SECTION
} while (TRUE);
```

The three properties ŌĆö Mutual Exclusion, Progress, and Bounded Waiting ŌĆö are all satisfied (proof as given in Question A).

#### Limitations [2 marks ŌĆö any two]

1. **Restricted to two processes** only. Cannot be directly extended to $n$ processes without complexity blow-up (filter lock / bakery algorithm required).
2. **Busy-wait / spinlock** ŌĆö wastes CPU cycles.
3. **Modern CPU issue**: Modern compilers may reorder statements and modern CPUs may execute out-of-order, breaking Peterson's logic unless **memory barriers** (`volatile`, `mb()`) are inserted.
4. **Cannot be used in preemptive kernels** without additional protection.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Initializing `empty` and `full` backwards**: `empty` starts at $N$, `full` starts at **0**, not the other way around. (ŌĆō1 to ŌĆō2 marks)
> 2. **Wrong order of `wait()` calls** in Producer-Consumer code. Always take `empty`/`full` BEFORE `mutex`. (ŌĆō2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting to make `wait()` and `signal()` atomic** in semaphore definitions. (ŌĆō1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing P()/V() with wait()/signal()** ŌĆö they are the same; writing both is fine but labelling them correctly is needed.
> 5. **In Peterson's proof**, students often write "by mutual exclusion, only one process can be in CS" ŌĆö this is circular reasoning. You must derive the contradiction from the variables, as shown above.
> 6. **Forgetting the circular indexing** `in = (in + 1) % N` in Producer-Consumer.
> 7. **Peterson's solution assumes sequential memory model** ŌĆö on modern out-of-order CPUs, it requires memory barriers; KTU may award bonus credit for mentioning this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ŌÜĪ Quick Revision Checklist

- **Race Condition**: Final outcome depends on interleaving of concurrent processes accessing shared data.
- **Critical Section (CS)**: Code segment accessing shared resources; needs exclusive access.
- **Three CS Requirements**: **M-E-P-B-W** ŌĆö **M**utual **E**xclusion, **P**rogress, **B**ounded **W**aiting.
- **Peterson's Variables**: `flag[2]` (intent) + `turn` (alternation resolver).
- **Peterson's ME Proof**: Both in CS ŌćÆ `turn = 0 AND turn = 1` ŌćÆ contradiction.
- **Peterson's Bound**: Bounded waiting constant $K = 1$.
- **Peterson's Limitations**: 2-process only, busy-wait, needs memory barriers on modern CPUs.
- **Semaphore** (Dijkstra, 1965): Integer accessed only via atomic `wait()` (P) and `signal()` (V).
- **Binary Semaphore**: Value Ōłł {0,1}; used for mutex.
- **Counting Semaphore**: Value Ōłł ŌäżŌēź0; used for resource pools.
- **Producer-Consumer Semaphores**: `empty = N`, `full = 0`, `mutex = 1`.
- **PC Order Rule**: Resource-count semaphore (`empty`/`full`) **before** `mutex`.
- **Circular Buffer Indices**: `in = (in + 1) % N`, `out = (out + 1) % N`.
- **Atomicity**: All `wait()` and `signal()` are atomic ŌĆö guaranteed via hardware (TSL/CAS) or kernel.
- **Deadlock Avoidance in PC**: Never hold `mutex` while waiting for `empty` or `full`.

### ­¤¦Ā Mnemonics

- **CS Requirements**: **"ME-P-BW"** ŌåÆ *"**M**y **E**ggs **P**roduce **B**ig **W**orlds"*
- **Semaphore Initialization**: **"E-N, F-Z, M-1"** ŌåÆ *Empty = N, Full = Zero, Mutex = 1*
- **Peterson's Three Steps**: **"FTW"** ŌåÆ *Flag set, Turn assigned, While wait*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resource Allocation Graphs - Deadlock Avoidance - Banker's Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Resource Allocation Graphs - Deadlock Avoidance - Banker's Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
A *Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)* is the bipartite directed graph model used by an Operating System to represent the precise instantaneous state of system resources and process-resource interactions. It is the **canonical graphical tool** for visualizing *deadlock existence* in a system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A RAG contains a **set of vertices V** and a **set of edges E**. The vertices are partitioned into two disjoint sets:
> - **P = {PŌéü, PŌéé, ..., PŌéÖ}** ŌĆö the set consisting of all active **processes** in the system.
> - **R = {RŌéü, RŌéé, ..., RŌéś}** ŌĆö the set consisting of all **resource types** in the system.
>
> A directed edge from process PßĄó to resource type RŌ▒╝ (PßĄó ŌåÆ RŌ▒╝) is a **request edge**. A directed edge from RŌ▒╝ to PßĄó (RŌ▒╝ ŌåÆ PßĄó) is an **assignment edge**.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Shared Workshop":**
Imagine a college engineering workshop with **4 lathes** and **3 students** working on projects.
- A *process* (student) sometimes requests a lathe ŌåÆ drawn as an arrow **from the student to the lathe** (request edge).
- When the student returns the lathe, the arrow reverses ŌåÆ becomes an **assignment edge** (lathe is currently *with* the student).
- If every lathe is being held by students, and every student is waiting for another lathe ŌåÆ **we have a circular wait, i.e., a deadlock** (the workshop is frozen!).

The RAG is simply the **"freeze-frame snapshot"** of who holds what and who is waiting for what at any moment.

> [!NOTE]
> **RAG with Single Instance vs. Multiple Instances:**
> - **Single-instance resources** ŌåÆ RAG can *definitively* detect deadlock (a cycle *implies* a deadlock).
> - **Multiple-instance resources** ŌåÆ RAG may show cycles that are **NOT deadlocks**. Here, the **Banker's Algorithm** must be used.

---

### 1.2 Deadlock Avoidance

**Formal Definition:**
*Deadlock Avoidance* requires that the operating system be given **advance information** about the *maximum* number of resources of each type that *every* process may ever request. Using this *a priori* knowledge, the OS dynamically decides whether the current resource allocation could **lead to a deadlock in the future**, and if so, refuses the request ŌĆö even if the request is currently safe to grant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Famous Deadlock Avoidance Algorithm = Banker's Algorithm** (proposed by Edsger W. Dijkstra, 1965). The name comes from the analogy of a banker who never allocates cash in a way that prevents lending to all customers.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Cautious Banker":**
A banker has a fixed amount of cash. Customers declare the **maximum loan** they might ever need. The banker grants a smaller loan only if he can still satisfy the *maximum need* of *every other customer* with the remaining cash. If granting the loan would risk an unsafe state, the banker **makes the customer wait**, even though technically cash is free.

---

### 1.3 Banker's Algorithm ŌĆö Core Idea

**Formal Definition:**
The Banker's Algorithm tests every hypothetical allocation against a set of *safety conditions*. A state is **safe** if there exists *some* sequence of process execution (a *safe sequence*) in which all processes can complete even when requesting their maximum resources. If no such sequence exists, the state is **unsafe**, and the corresponding request must be **denied**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Fundamental State Concepts (KTU must-know):**
>
> | State | Meaning |
> |-------|---------|
> | **Safe State** | A safe sequence exists; no deadlock guaranteed. |
> | **Unsafe State** | No guarantee of safe sequence; **possible** deadlock. |
> | **Deadlocked State** | Certain deadlock ŌĆö at least one process is permanently blocked. |
>
> **Safe ŌŖé Non-Deadlocked ŌŖå All States.** Deadlock Avoidance = **avoidance of unsafe states**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->
