# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōØ Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]**
**Differentiate between Internal Fragmentation and External Fragmentation. In which memory allocation scheme does each occur predominantly?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Internal Fragmentation | External Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Unused memory *inside* an allocated partition (waste within block). | Free memory exists in scattered holes, but no single hole is large enough for a request. |
| **Predominant Scheme** | **Fixed (Static) Partitioning** | **Variable (Dynamic) Partitioning** |
| **Cause** | Partition size > Process size | Processes leave behind small non-contiguous holes |
| **Solution** | Smarter partition sizing | Compaction, Paging, Segmentation |
| **Example** | 200 KB partition holding 170 KB process ŌåÆ 30 KB wasted. | Total free 500 KB in five 100 KB holes; process needs 200 KB ŌåÆ rejected. |

> **Marking:** 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for scheme mapping. Examiner will not award full marks if no example is given.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**
**Explain the working of the Base and Limit Registers in contiguous memory allocation. Why is the limit register check essential for system security?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Base Register** stores the starting physical address of a process's allocated contiguous block, while the **Limit Register** stores the size (length) of that block.

**Address Translation (1 mark):**
$$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Base} + \text{Logical Address}$$

**Protection Mechanism (1 mark):**
The MMU verifies `0 Ōēż Logical Address < Limit` before translation. If the condition fails, a **trap** is raised.

**Security Importance (1 mark):**
The limit register prevents a buggy or malicious process from accessing memory belonging to the OS kernel or other processes ŌĆö this enforces **memory isolation**, the foundation of multi-user security. Without it, any process could read/write arbitrary RAM, crashing the entire system or stealing sensitive data.

---

## ­¤ōØ Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Marks are awarded ONLY when the candidate draws a clear memory snapshot diagram, shows step-wise search decisions, and explicitly names the strategy used. Generic paragraphs without a table or diagram fetch only 50% marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Pattern]*

Consider a system with **1024 KB** of RAM. After several allocations and deallocations, the free memory holes (in order from low to high address) are: **200 KB, 400 KB, 150 KB, 300 KB, 250 KB**. New process requests arrive as:

| Process | Memory Required |
|---|---|
| P1 | 220 KB |
| P2 | 350 KB |
| P3 | 90 KB |
| P4 | 280 KB |
| P5 | 410 KB |

**Solve the following:**

**(a)** Show the memory layout after all possible allocations under **First-Fit**. Calculate **Total Free Memory, External Fragmentation, and the Number of Holes.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** If the same sequence of requests is processed under **Best-Fit**, show the resulting memory layout. Compare the number of holes and external fragmentation with First-Fit, and recommend which strategy is preferable for this workload. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) ŌĆö First-Fit (7 marks)**

**Initial Hole List:** `[200, 400, 150, 300, 250]` (in KB, low-to-high order)

| Step | Process | Size | Strategy | Hole Chosen | New Hole List |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 220 | First-Fit (scan from start) | **400** (first Ōēź 220) | [200, **180**, 150, 300, 250] |
| 2 | P2 | 350 | First-Fit | **None** (no hole Ōēź 350) | Unchanged |
| 3 | P3 | 90 | First-Fit | **200** (first Ōēź 90) | [**110**, 180, 150, 300, 250] |
| 4 | P4 | 280 | First-Fit | **300** (first Ōēź 280) | [110, 180, 150, **20**, 250] |
| 5 | P5 | 410 | First-Fit | **None** | Unchanged |

**Final Memory Layout Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM["RAM 1024 KB"]
        H1["Hole 110 KB"]
        P3["P3 = 90 KB"]
        H2["Hole 180 KB"]
        H3["Hole 150 KB"]
        P4["P4 = 280 KB"]
        H4["Hole 20 KB"]
        H5["Hole 250 KB"]
        ALLOC1["P1 = 220 KB"]
        ALLOC2["P2 REJECTED"]
        ALLOC3["P5 REJECTED"]
    end
    H1 --- P3
    P3 --- H2
    H2 --- H3
    H3 --- P4
    P4 --- H4
    H4 --- H5
```

**Fragmentation Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Free Memory} &= 110 + 180 + 150 + 20 + 250 = 710 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Largest Single Hole} &= 250 \text{ KB} \\
\text{External Fragmentation} &= 710 - 250 = 460 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Number of Holes} &= 5 \\
\text{Processes Allocated} &= 3 \text{ out of } 5 \quad (\text{P1, P3, P4}) \\
\text{Processes Rejected} &= 2 \quad (\text{P2, P5})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) ŌĆö Best-Fit (7 marks)**

**Best-Fit sorts the hole list ascending for each search.**

| Step | Process | Size | Sorted Holes (Ascending) | Smallest Hole Ōēź Request | Resulting List |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 220 | [150, 200, 250, 300, 400] | **250** | [150, 200, **30**, 300, 400] |
| 2 | P2 | 350 | [30, 150, 200, 300, 400] | **400** | [30, 150, 200, 300, **50**] |
| 3 | P3 | 90 | [30, 50, 150, 200, 300] | **150** | [30, 50, **60**, 200, 300] |
| 4 | P4 | 280 | [30, 50, 60, 200, 300] | **300** | [30, 50, 60, 200, **20**] |
| 5 | P5 | 410 | [20, 30, 50, 60, 200] | **None** | Unchanged |

**Fragmentation Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Free Memory} &= 30 + 50 + 60 + 200 + 20 = 360 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Largest Single Hole} &= 200 \text{ KB} \\
\text{External Fragmentation} &= 360 - 200 = 160 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Number of Holes} &= 5 \\
\text{Processes Allocated} &= 4 \text{ out of } 5 \quad (\text{P1, P2, P3, P4}) \\
\text{Processes Rejected} &= 1 \quad (\text{P5})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparative Analysis (2 marks):**

| Metric | First-Fit | Best-Fit | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Free Memory | 710 KB | 360 KB | First-Fit (less wasted per allocation) |
| External Fragmentation | 460 KB | 160 KB | **Best-Fit (less EF)** |
| Processes Allocated | 3 / 5 | 4 / 5 | **Best-Fit** |
| Speed | Fastest | Slowest | First-Fit |
| Hole Splinters | Few | Many | First-Fit |

**Recommendation:** For this specific workload, **Best-Fit** is preferable because it allocates one additional process (P2 successfully) and produces 65% less external fragmentation. However, in a system where allocation speed matters, First-Fit remains the practical choice.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Pattern ŌĆō Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Describe **Fixed Partitioning** and **Variable Partitioning** schemes for contiguous memory allocation. Compare them in a tabular form with at least 6 criteria. With a suitable diagram, explain how **Compaction** solves external fragmentation, and state **two limitations** of compaction. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following initial free hole list (in KB): **300, 600, 350, 200, 500**. Process arrivals are:

| Process | Size (KB) |
|---|---|
| A | 275 |
| B | 400 |
| C | 150 |
| D | 550 |

Apply **Worst-Fit** allocation. After all allocations, calculate the **Total Free Memory, External Fragmentation, and the largest single hole** remaining. If compaction is then performed, what is the new single hole size? *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Fixed Partitioning (2 marks):**
- Memory is divided into a fixed number of partitions of equal (or unequal) sizes at system boot time.
- Each partition holds exactly **one process**.
- Suffers from **Internal Fragmentation** if a process is smaller than its partition.
- Limitation: degree of multiprogramming is fixed at boot; cannot adapt to workload.

**Variable Partitioning (2 marks):**
- Partitions are created dynamically, sized exactly to the process.
- No internal fragmentation, but **external fragmentation** occurs over time.
- More flexible; degree of multiprogramming varies dynamically.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Criterion | Fixed Partitioning | Variable Partitioning |
|---|---|---|
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Internal Fragmentation | Yes | No |
| External Fragmentation | No (but unused partitions) | Yes |
| Partition Count | Fixed at boot | Dynamic |
| Implementation Complexity | Simple | Complex (allocation strategies needed) |
| Compaction Needed | No | Yes |
| Multiprogramming Level | Static | Dynamic |

**Compaction Diagram + Limitations (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE COMPACTION"]
        B1["OS"]
        B2["P1"]
        B3["H1"]
        B4["P2"]
        B5["H2"]
        B6["P3"]
        B7["H3"]
    end
    subgraph AFTER["AFTER COMPACTION"]
        A1["OS"]
        A2["P1"]
        A3["P2"]
        A4["P3"]
        A5["MERGED HOLE 800 KB"]
    end
    BEFORE -.SHUFFLE.-> AFTER

    style B1 fill:#34495E,color:#fff
    style B3 fill:#E74C3C,color:#fff
    style B5 fill:#E74C3C,color:#fff
    style B7 fill:#E74C3C,color:#fff
    style A5 fill:#27AE60,color:#fff
```

**Two Limitations of Compaction:**
1. **CPU-intensive** ŌĆö moving large memory blocks consumes significant processor time, degrading system performance.
2. **I/O conflict** ŌĆö if a process is performing I/O, its buffers are pinned to physical addresses; moving them invalidates DMA pointers and device registers.

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) ŌĆö Worst-Fit (7 marks)**

**Initial Holes:** `[300, 600, 350, 200, 500]` KB

| Step | Process | Size | Sorted Holes (Descending) | Largest Hole | New Holes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | 275 | [600, 500, 350, 300, 200] | **600** | [500, 350, 300, 200, **325**] |
| 2 | B | 400 | [500, 350, 325, 300, 200] | **500** | [350, 325, 300, 200, **100**] |
| 3 | C | 150 | [350, 325, 300, 200, 100] | **350** | [325, 300, 200, 100, **200**] |
| 4 | D | 550 | [325, 300, 200, 200, 100] | **None** (all < 550) | Unchanged ŌĆö Rejected |

**Final Calculations (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Holes} &= [325, 300, 200, 200, 100] \text{ KB} \\
\text{Total Free Memory} &= 325 + 300 + 200 + 200 + 100 = 1125 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Largest Single Hole} &= 325 \text{ KB} \\
\text{External Fragmentation} &= 1125 - 325 = 800 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Processes Allocated} &= 3 \text{ out of } 4 \quad (\text{A, B, C}) \\
\text{Processes Rejected} &= 1 \quad (\text{D, needs 550 KB})
\end{aligned}
$$

**After Compaction (2 marks):**
Compaction merges all scattered holes into one contiguous block:

$$
\text{New Single Hole} = 325 + 300 + 200 + 200 + 100 = 1125 \text{ KB}
$$

Now process **D (550 KB) can be allocated** from this new merged hole, leaving a remainder of `1125 ŌĆō 550 = 575 KB`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning ŌĆö Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting to **sort the holes** before applying Best-Fit or Worst-Fit (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Confusing *Total Free Memory* with *External Fragmentation* ŌĆö they are NOT the same. EF = Total Free ŌĆō Largest Hole.
> 3. Not showing the **memory layout diagram** for full marks on part (a) of 7-mark questions.
> 4. Failing to state explicitly that compaction **requires halting processes** (or expensive live relocation using registers).
> 5. For **Base/Limit questions**, students often write `PA = Base + Limit` ŌĆö WRONG. Correct is `PA = Base + Logical Address`.

---

## ­¤öü Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)**

- **Definition:** Contiguous Memory Allocation = Each process gets **one single continuous block** of physical RAM.
- **Two Schemes:** *Fixed (Static)* ŌåÆ Internal Fragmentation; *Variable (Dynamic)* ŌåÆ External Fragmentation.
- **Four Allocation Strategies:** *First-Fit* (fastest), *Best-Fit* (minimum leftover), *Worst-Fit* (largest remainder), *Next-Fit* (circular).
- **Best-Fit** produces smallest leftover holes, **but** generates the most splinters.
- **First-Fit** is the fastest and is used in many real OS (e.g., early Unix).
- **Internal Fragmentation = Partition Size ŌłÆ Process Size.**
- **External Fragmentation = Total Free Memory ŌłÆ Largest Single Hole.**
- **50% Rule (Statistic):** On average, with First-Fit, one-third of memory becomes unusable due to external fragmentation (this is an examiner-favorite numerical fact).
- **Compaction** is the only software solution for external fragmentation without changing the allocation scheme.
- **Address Translation Formula:** `PA = Base + LA`. Protection: `0 Ōēż LA < Limit`.
- **Base Register** = starting physical address; **Limit Register** = size of allocated block.
- **MMU** is the hardware that performs the translation; failure triggers a **trap** (segmentation fault).
- **Solutions to External Fragmentation:** (1) Compaction, (2) Paging (Module 5), (3) Segmentation.
- **Why CMA fails at scale:** As processes come and go, holes scatter; even with abundant total free memory, a large process may be rejected.
- **Golden rule for KTU numerics:** Always show the *initial hole list*, the *strategy name*, the *chosen hole*, the *new hole list*, and finally the *fragmentation table*.
- **Memory Protection is mandatory** ŌĆö without the limit check, one buggy process can crash the kernel.

> **End of Module 4 ŌĆö Contiguous Memory Allocation Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PERUT638)**

topics.title: Fixed and Variable Partitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fixed and Variable Partitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Memory Management in Multiprogramming
Before a process can execute, the Operating System must allocate it a contiguous region of main memory. The two classical, contiguous allocation techniques taught under the KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 4 ŌĆō Memory Management) are **Fixed Partitions** and **Variable Partitions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Contiguous Memory Allocation** is a memory management scheme in which each process is allocated a single, continuous block of physical memory. **Fixed Partitioning** pre-divides memory into fixed-size blocks at system boot, whereas **Variable Partitioning** dynamically creates partitions whose sizes exactly match the requirements of incoming processes.

### 1.2 Fixed Partitions ŌĆō Formal Definition
In the **Fixed Partition (Static Partition)** scheme, the main memory is partitioned into a fixed number of **non-overlapping, fixed-size blocks** at the time of system generation or boot. The size and number of partitions are determined *before* any process is loaded and **cannot be changed at run time**. Only one process can be resident in one partition at any given instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristics of Fixed Partitions**
> - Number of partitions = Degree of multiprogramming (fixed).
> - The partition size is determined by the **system designer** (not the OS at runtime).
> - The OS maintains a separate **ready queue** for each partition (or a single queue searched sequentially).

### 1.3 Variable Partitions ŌĆō Formal Definition
In the **Variable Partition (Dynamic Partition)** scheme, there are **no pre-defined partitions**. When a process arrives, the OS allocates a block of memory **exactly equal to the process size**. As processes enter and leave the system, the available memory becomes a collection of holes of varying sizes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristics of Variable Partitions**
> - Partitions are created *on-demand* at runtime.
> - No internal fragmentation (waste inside an allocated block).
> - Suffers from **External Fragmentation** (free memory exists but is not contiguous).
> - Requires allocation strategies: **First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit**.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy ŌĆō The Library Study Hall
Imagine a University Library Reading Hall with **150 desks**.

- **Fixed Partition Analogy:** The hall is permanently divided by glass walls into rooms of fixed sizes ŌĆö Room A (10 desks), Room B (20 desks), Room C (30 desks), Room D (40 desks), Room E (50 desks). A study group of **15 students** can *only* be seated in Room B or larger; the extra 5 empty desks in Room B are **wasted** (this waste is *internal fragmentation*). If 4 groups are already using the larger rooms, the 5th group ŌĆö even if only 3 students ŌĆö may not be admitted because the *count of rooms* is fixed.

- **Variable Partition Analogy:** Instead of fixed walls, the librarian puts up *temporary* partitions of exact length required by each group. A group of 15 gets 15 desks walled off exactly. Over the day, as groups leave, small gaps of 4 desks, 7 desks, 12 desks appear scattered around the hall. A new group of 10 may be turned away even though the hall has 23 free desks in total, because no single *contiguous* gap can hold them (this is *external fragmentation*).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Visual Cue for Exams:** If you see the words "fixed size" ŌåÆ think *Internal Fragmentation*. If you see "variable size" or "exact fit" ŌåÆ think *External Fragmentation*.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics (Frequently Tested)
| Metric | Value/Unit |
|---|---|
| **Internal Fragmentation** | Measured in **Bytes / KB / MB** (wasted space inside a partition) |
| **External Fragmentation** | Measured as a **Percentage (%)** of total free memory that is unusable |
| **Fragmentation Threshold (50% Rule)** | $N$ allocations ŌåÆ $0.5 \times N$ blocks lost on average |
| **Memory Word Size** | Typically **32 bits or 64 bits** in modern systems |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fixed Partitions ŌĆō Operational Logic

**Step 1 ŌĆō System Initialization**
The system administrator, at boot time, fixes the number of partitions ($n$) and the size of each partition ($S_i$). The OS builds a **Partition Table** storing the base address and size of each partition.

**Step 2 ŌĆō Process Arrival**
A new process of size $P_{size}$ arrives. The OS searches the partition table for the *smallest* partition that can hold the process. If found, the process is loaded at the partition's base address and the partition is marked **BUSY**.

**Step 3 ŌĆō Process Termination**
When a process terminates, its partition is marked **FREE**. The OS may optionally coalesce adjacent free partitions (in some hybrid designs).

**Step 4 ŌĆō Multiprogramming Limit**
The number of concurrent processes is **hard-capped** by the number of partitions. If 5 partitions exist, at most 5 processes can be in memory ŌĆö even if memory is largely empty.

#### 2.1.1 Sub-Types of Fixed Partitions
- **Equal-Sized Partitions:** All $n$ partitions have identical size $S$. Causes severe *internal fragmentation* (a 1 KB process in a 100 KB partition wastes 99 KB). Simple to implement; uses a single ready queue.
- **Unequal-Sized Partitions:** Partitions vary in size (e.g., one small, one medium, one large). Reduces internal fragmentation; the OS uses **separate ready queues** (one per partition) and assigns processes to the smallest partition that fits.

### 2.2 Variable Partitions ŌĆō Operational Logic

**Step 1 ŌĆō Free List Maintenance**
The OS maintains a **linked list of holes** (free memory blocks) with their base addresses and sizes.

**Step 2 ŌĆō Allocation Request**
A process of size $P_{size}$ requests memory. The OS traverses the free list using a chosen strategy and selects a hole $H$ such that $H_{size} \geq P_{size}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆō Hole Splitting**
The selected hole is split into two:
- An **allocated block** of exactly $P_{size}$ bytes.
- A **residual hole** of size $H_{size} - P_{size}$ (added back to the free list, if non-zero).

**Step 4 ŌĆō Hole Coalescing**
When a process releases memory, the OS checks the neighbours of the newly freed block. If adjacent blocks are also free, they are **merged (coalesced)** into a single larger hole to combat external fragmentation.

**Step 5 ŌĆō Compaction (Optional, Heavy)**
At any time, the OS may trigger **compaction** ŌĆö sliding all allocated blocks to one end of memory to merge scattered holes into a single large free block. Compaction is **I/O intensive** and requires dynamic relocation support.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Internal Fragmentation (Fixed Partition) | $IF = S_{partition} - P_{size}$ | Bytes (per process) |
| 2 | Average Internal Fragmentation | $\overline{IF} = \frac{S_{partition}}{2}$ (for uniform random process sizes) | Bytes |
| 3 | External Fragmentation | $EF = \text{Total Free Memory} - \text{Largest Contiguous Hole}$ | Bytes (unusable) |
| 4 | External Fragmentation Percentage | $EF\% = \frac{EF}{\text{Total Free Memory}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 5 | 50% Rule (First-Fit) | Average lost memory $= \frac{1}{3} \times N \times \text{avg hole size}$ | Knuth's bound |
| 6 | Number of Holes Created (First-Fit) | $N$ allocations ŌåÆ approx $N/2$ holes remain | Statistical |
| 7 | Memory Utilization | $U = \frac{\sum P_{size}}{\text{Total Memory}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 8 | Multiprogramming Degree (Fixed) | $D_{mp} = n_{partitions}$ | Integer |
| 9 | Multiprogramming Degree (Variable) | $D_{mp} = \lfloor \text{Total Memory} \;/\; \overline{P_{size}} \rfloor$ | Integer (theoretical max) |
| 10 | Compaction Time | $T_{compaction} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} 2 \times t_{copy,i}$ | Depends on memory copy speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Reminder:** In Fixed Partitions, ALWAYS check whether the question specifies **Equal** or **Unequal** size partitions. The fragmentation answer changes drastically between the two.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Fixed Partitions** were used in early mainframe OS such as **IBM OS/360 MFT (Multiprogramming with a Fixed number of Tasks)**. The simplicity allowed predictable batch scheduling.
- **Variable Partitions** are the conceptual ancestor of modern **slab allocators** in the Linux kernel (kmalloc), **Java Virtual Machine (JVM) heap management (generational GC)**, and **Windows 9x memory manager** (although modern OS use paging, the contiguous allocation math is identical at the page level).
- The fragmentation mathematics derived here is also applied in **disk storage allocation (FAT, contiguous file allocation)** and **embedded systems flash management**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Fixed Partitions (Unequal Size)

**Problem Statement:**
Main memory size = **100 KB**. The OS creates the following fixed partitions:
- P1: 10 KB
- P2: 20 KB
- P3: 30 KB
- P4: 40 KB

The following processes arrive in order: **PA = 7 KB, PB = 25 KB, PC = 15 KB, PD = 35 KB, PE = 8 KB**.
Using the *smallest-fit* rule, allocate each process and compute the total internal fragmentation.

**Step-by-Step Allocation:**

| Arrival | Process Size | Smallest Partition that Fits | Allocated? | Internal Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PA | 7 KB | P1 (10 KB) | Ō£ģ Yes ŌåÆ P1 | 10 - 7 = **3 KB** |
| PB | 25 KB | P3 (30 KB) | Ō£ģ Yes ŌåÆ P3 | 30 - 25 = **5 KB** |
| PC | 15 KB | P2 (20 KB) | Ō£ģ Yes ŌåÆ P2 | 20 - 15 = **5 KB** |
| PD | 35 KB | P4 (40 KB) | Ō£ģ Yes ŌåÆ P4 | 40 - 35 = **5 KB** |
| PE | 8 KB | None (all busy) | ŌØī Must wait | ŌĆö |

**Total Internal Fragmentation** (after all four allocations):

$$\begin{aligned}
IF_{total} &= IF_{PA} + IF_{PB} + IF_{PC} + IF_{PD} \\
&= 3 + 5 + 5 + 5 \\
&= 18 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Even though the total process demand is 82 KB and total memory is 100 KB, 18 KB is *unavoidably* wasted as internal fragmentation. Process PE cannot run until one of the four partitions is freed.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Variable Partitions (First-Fit)

**Problem Statement:**
Memory of **256 KB** initially contains three holes and two allocated blocks. Free list (in address order):
- Hole H1: 20 KB @ 0 KB
- Allocated A1: 60 KB @ 20 KB
- Hole H2: 50 KB @ 80 KB
- Allocated A2: 100 KB @ 130 KB
- Hole H3: 26 KB @ 230 KB

Process **PX = 30 KB** arrives. Apply **First-Fit**, **Best-Fit**, and **Worst-Fit** and find the resulting free list and external fragmentation.

**Step-by-Step Solution (First-Fit):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Scan free list from H1:} \quad & H1 = 20 \text{ KB} < 30 \text{ KB} \rightarrow \text{Reject} \\
& H2 = 50 \text{ KB} \geq 30 \text{ KB} \rightarrow \text{Accept} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Allocate PX in H2. Split H2:
- Allocated PX: 30 KB @ 80 KB
- New Hole H2': 20 KB @ 110 KB

Updated free list:
- H1: 20 KB @ 0 KB
- H2': 20 KB @ 110 KB
- H3: 26 KB @ 230 KB

**External Fragmentation Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
EF_{bytes} &= \text{Total Free Memory} - \text{Largest Contiguous Hole} \\
&= (20 + 20 + 26) - 26 \\
&= 66 - 26 \\
&= 40 \text{ KB} \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
EF_{\%} &= \frac{EF_{bytes}}{\text{Total Free Memory}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{40}{66} \times 100 \\
&\approx 60.61\% \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-Step Solution (Best-Fit):**
Smallest hole that fits = H2 (50 KB is smaller than 26 KB? No, 26 KB < 50 KB, so 26 KB cannot hold 30 KB).
So check: H1 = 20 KB (No), H2 = 50 KB (Yes), H3 = 26 KB (No). **Best-Fit also chooses H2.** Result identical to First-Fit for this case.

**Step-by-Step Solution (Worst-Fit):**
Largest hole = H2 (50 KB). Allocate here, split: 30 KB used + 20 KB residual. **Result identical to First-Fit** because H2 happens to be the largest.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** When one hole is *simultaneously* the first, the smallest-fitting, and the largest, all three strategies produce the same outcome. State this explicitly in the answer to score full marks.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favorite)

| Parameter | Fixed Partition | Variable Partition |
|---|---|---|
| Partition Size | Pre-defined, **constant** | **Dynamic**, matches process |
| Partition Number | **Fixed** at boot | **Variable** (Ōēż number of processes) |
| Internal Fragmentation | **Yes (significant)** | **None** |
| External Fragmentation | **No** | **Yes (significant)** |
| Implementation Complexity | **Low** (simple table) | **High** (free list, coalescing) |
| Degree of Multiprogramming | **Bounded** by partition count | **Bounded** by total memory |
| Memory Utilization | **Poor to Moderate** | **Good (without compaction)** |
| Compaction Required | **No** | **Yes (periodically)** |
| Overhead | **Minimal** | **Higher (bookkeeping)** |
| Used In | **IBM MFT, early mainframes** | **IBM MVT, early UNIX variants** |
| Best Use Case | Predictable batch systems | General-purpose time-sharing |

### 3.4 Knuth's 50% Rule Derivation (Conceptual)

For **First-Fit** allocation, Knuth proved that approximately **one-third of the allocated blocks** are in the *last* third of the address space. This implies that, statistically, **half of the holes** tend to be too small to satisfy typical requests.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Avg. unusable memory} &= \frac{1}{3} \times N \times \overline{H_{size}} \\
\text{Avg. usable memory} &= \frac{1}{2} \times N \times \overline{H_{size}} \\
\text{Waste ratio} &\approx \frac{1/3}{1/2 + 1/3} = \frac{2}{5} = 40\% \\
\end{aligned}$$

This 40% figure is the classical justification for **paging** in modern systems ŌĆö the textbook motivation that led to virtual memory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fixed Partition Memory Layout (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RAM["MAIN MEMORY 100 KB"]
        direction LR
        P1["PARTITION 1 : 10 KB"]:::busy
        P2["PARTITION 2 : 20 KB"]:::busy
        P3["PARTITION 3 : 30 KB"]:::busy
        P4["PARTITION 4 : 40 KB"]:::busy
        OS["OS RESIDENT : 10 KB"]:::os
    end
    OS ~~~ P1
    P1 ~~~ P2
    P2 ~~~ P3
    P3 ~~~ P4

    classDef os fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef busy fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Variable Partition Memory Layout with External Fragmentation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RAM2["MAIN MEMORY 256 KB"]
        direction LR
        A1["ALLOCATED A1 : 60 KB"]:::busy
        H1["HOLE 20 KB"]:::free
        PX["ALLOCATED PX : 30 KB"]:::busy
        H2["HOLE 20 KB"]:::free
        A2["ALLOCATED A2 : 100 KB"]:::busy
        H3["HOLE 26 KB"]:::free
    end
    H1 --> PX
    PX --> H2
    A1 --- H1
    A2 --- H3

    classDef busy fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef free fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Allocation Strategy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Process Request Arrives of Size S"] --> B{"Partition Type?"}
    B -- "FIXED" --> C["Look up Partition Table"]
    C --> D{"Smallest partition that fits?"}
    D -- "YES" --> E["Load Process, Mark BUSY"]
    D -- "NO" --> F["Add to Ready Queue of that class"]
    B -- "VARIABLE" --> G["Scan Free List"]
    G --> H{"Which Strategy?"}
    H -- "First-Fit" --> I["Pick FIRST hole >= S"]
    H -- "Best-Fit" --> J["Pick SMALLEST hole >= S"]
    H -- "Worst-Fit" --> K["Pick LARGEST hole >= S"]
    I --> L["Split Hole: Allocate S, Retain residual"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M["Update Free List"]
    M --> N{"Adjacent holes after release?"}
    N -- "YES" --> O["Coalesce Holes"]
    N -- "NO" --> P["Wait for Next Request"]
```

### 4.4 Process of Compaction (Variable Partitions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE COMPACTION"]
        direction LR
        B1["HOLE 10"]:::free
        B2["A1 60"]:::busy
        B3["HOLE 20"]:::free
        B4["A2 100"]:::busy
        B5["HOLE 26"]:::free
    end
    BEFORE -- "OS shifts all allocated blocks left" --> AFTER

    subgraph AFTER["AFTER COMPACTION"]
        direction LR
        A1n["A1 60"]:::busy
        A2n["A2 100"]:::busy
        A3n["HOLE 56"]:::free
    end

    classDef busy fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef free fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this carefully:** The total free memory is **identical** before and after compaction (56 KB). What changes is the *number of holes* ŌĆö from **3 scattered holes** to **1 contiguous hole**. This is precisely why compaction solves *external* (not internal) fragmentation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
Differentiate between **internal fragmentation** and **external fragmentation**. In which type of partition scheme does each occur?

**Model Answer:**
- **Internal Fragmentation:** Wasted memory *inside* an allocated partition because the partition is larger than the process loaded into it. Occurs in **Fixed Partition** schemes.
- **External Fragmentation:** Wasted memory that exists as small, non-contiguous holes between allocated blocks; total free memory is sufficient but no single hole is large enough. Occurs in **Variable Partition** schemes.
- *One-liner memory hook:* "Internal = inside the box, External = outside the box (scattered)."

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Sessional II]**
List any **three advantages** of variable partitioning over fixed partitioning.

**Model Answer:**
1. **No internal fragmentation** ŌĆö partition size exactly matches process size.
2. **Higher degree of multiprogramming** is possible since partition count is not fixed.
3. **Better memory utilization** when process sizes vary widely; no space is reserved for partitions that may go unused.
*(Optional 4th point: Supports more flexible scheduling of mixed workloads.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Fixed Partition Focus**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Fixed Partition** memory management scheme with a neat diagram. Discuss the difference between **equal-size** and **unequal-size** partitions.

**Model Solution:**

*Definition:* Fixed Partitioning divides main memory into a fixed number of non-overlapping, fixed-size blocks at system boot time. The OS maintains a partition table with the base address and limit of each partition.

*Diagram (refer Section 4.1):* OS code | P1 (10 KB) | P2 (20 KB) | P3 (30 KB) | P4 (40 KB).

*Equal-Size Partitions:*
- All partitions have identical size $S$.
- Simple: one ready queue, one search.
- Severe internal fragmentation if process sizes are non-uniform (e.g., 1 KB process in 50 KB partition wastes 49 KB).
- Multiprogramming degree = $n$ (number of partitions), independent of process size.

*Unequal-Size Partitions:*
- Partition sizes vary (e.g., 10, 20, 30, 40 KB).
- Separate ready queue per partition.
- Process is loaded into the *smallest* partition that can contain it ŌåÆ reduced internal fragmentation.
- A small process doesn't block a large partition; a large process cannot enter a small partition.
- Disadvantage: a large job may still not run if all big partitions are busy with smaller jobs (queue starvation possible).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A system has memory of **500 KB** divided into **5 fixed partitions of sizes 50, 100, 150, 100, 100 KB**. Processes arrive in the order P1 = 80 KB, P2 = 120 KB, P3 = 45 KB, P4 = 95 KB, P5 = 70 KB. Show the allocation using the **best-fit partition rule** and compute the **total internal fragmentation**.

**Model Solution:**

| Process | Size | Best-Fit Partition Chosen | Partition Size | Internal Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 80 KB | Second (100 KB) | 100 KB | 100 - 80 = 20 KB |
| P2 | 120 KB | Third (150 KB) | 150 KB | 150 - 120 = 30 KB |
| P3 | 45 KB | First (50 KB) | 50 KB | 50 - 45 = 5 KB |
| P4 | 95 KB | Fourth (100 KB) | 100 KB | 100 - 95 = 5 KB |
| P5 | 70 KB | Fifth (100 KB) | 100 KB | 100 - 70 = 30 KB |

*(P5 cannot enter P2's 150 KB partition because P2 is already loaded there.)*

$$\begin{aligned}
IF_{total} &= 20 + 30 + 5 + 5 + 30 \\
&= 90 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **forget to subtract already-occupied partitions** when selecting the "best fit." If P1 occupies the 100 KB partition, then for P2 you must exclude it and choose the *next* smallest fitting partition. Marks are deducted for using a partition that is already busy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Variable Partition Focus**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **First-Fit, Best-Fit, and Worst-Fit** allocation strategies for variable partitions. Compare their performance in terms of **fragmentation** and **search time**.

**Model Solution:**

*First-Fit:*
- Scans the free list from the beginning and allocates the **first hole** that is large enough.
- **Fastest** (on average, only the first few holes are examined).
- Tends to leave small holes at the front of memory; large holes remain at the end.
- Knuth's 50% Rule: ~1/3 of memory is wasted on average.

*Best-Fit:*
- Scans the *entire* free list and allocates the **smallest hole** that is large enough.
- Minimizes the size of the *residual* hole (good for utilizing small holes).
- **Slowest** (full list scan).
- Creates many tiny unusable holes ŌåÆ *worse external fragmentation* in practice.

*Worst-Fit:*
- Scans the entire free list and allocates the **largest hole**.
- Leaves the *largest possible* residual hole, hoping it remains usable.
- **Slowest** (full list scan) and generally **worst** in empirical studies.
- Useful only when most requests are for small blocks.

*Comparison Table:*

| Strategy | Search Time | Residual Hole Size | Empirical Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|---|
| First-Fit | **Fast** | Variable | Moderate |
| Best-Fit | Slow | **Smallest** | Worst (many tiny holes) |
| Worst-Fit | Slow | Largest | Moderate |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given a memory of **400 KB** with the following sequence of free blocks (in order): **Hole A = 60 KB, Hole B = 100 KB, Hole C = 30 KB, Hole D = 200 KB**. Processes arrive in order: **P1 = 75 KB, P2 = 150 KB, P3 = 25 KB, P4 = 90 KB, P5 = 60 KB**. Show the allocation using **Best-Fit** and compute the **final external fragmentation**.

**Model Solution:**

*Initial free list:* A(60), B(100), C(30), D(200).

**Allocate P1 = 75 KB (Best-Fit):**
- A=60 < 75 ŌØī
- B=100 Ōēź 75 Ō£ģ (smallest fitting = B)

After allocation, free list: A(60), C(30), D(200), B' = 25 KB (residual of B).

**Allocate P2 = 150 KB (Best-Fit):**
- A=60 < 150 ŌØī
- C=30 < 150 ŌØī
- B'=25 < 150 ŌØī
- D=200 Ōēź 150 Ō£ģ

After allocation, free list: A(60), C(30), B'(25), D' = 50 KB (residual of D).

**Allocate P3 = 25 KB (Best-Fit):**
- A=60 Ōēź 25 Ō£ģ (smallest fitting = A... but wait, B' = 25 KB fits exactly!)

Best-Fit chooses **B' (25 KB)** as it is the smallest hole that fits.

After allocation, free list: A(60), C(30), D'(50). B is gone.

**Allocate P4 = 90 KB (Best-Fit):**
- A=60 < 90 ŌØī
- C=30 < 90 ŌØī
- D'=50 < 90 ŌØī
- No hole fits ŌåÆ **P4 must wait or be denied.**

*State after P4 rejection:* Free list = A(60), C(30), D'(50). Allocated = P1 in B(100), P2 in D(200), P3 in B'(25).

**Allocate P5 = 60 KB (Best-Fit):**
- A=60 Ōēź 60 Ō£ģ (exact fit)

After allocation, free list: C(30), D'(50). A is gone.

**Final Free Memory State:**
- Total free memory = 30 + 50 = 80 KB
- Largest contiguous hole = 50 KB
- Number of holes = 2

$$\begin{aligned}
EF_{bytes} &= \text{Total Free} - \text{Largest Hole} \\
&= 80 - 50 \\
&= 30 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
EF_{\%} &= \frac{30}{80} \times 100 = 37.5\% \\
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In Best-Fit, students frequently **miss the residual hole** of the *just-allocated* partition. For example, after giving P3 the entire 25 KB hole B', the residual is 0 (not a new hole). Always update the free list *immediately* after each allocation. Marks are lost when the free list shown is inconsistent with the allocations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4**

- Ō£ģ **Fixed Partition** ŌåÆ partitions defined at **boot time**, sizes are **constants**, internal fragmentation is **guaranteed**.
- Ō£ģ **Variable Partition** ŌåÆ partitions **created on demand**, sizes match process exactly, external fragmentation is the problem.
- Ō£ģ **Equal vs Unequal Fixed:** Unequal partitions use **multiple ready queues** (one per size class); equal partitions use **one queue**.
- Ō£ģ **First-Fit** is the *fastest* and most commonly used; **Best-Fit** is theoretically "optimal" but creates the *worst* tiny holes in practice; **Worst-Fit** is rarely used.
- Ō£ģ **Coalescing** joins adjacent free blocks; **Compaction** slides allocated blocks to merge all holes.
- Ō£ģ **Knuth's 50% Rule** states that after $N$ First-Fit allocations, about $N/2$ holes remain, and ~1/3 of memory is statistically lost ŌĆö this is the formal motivation for **paging**.
- Ō£ģ **Formula must-memorize:**
  - $IF = S_{partition} - P_{size}$
  - $EF_{bytes} = \text{Total Free} - \text{Largest Hole}$
  - $EF_{\%} = \frac{EF_{bytes}}{\text{Total Free}} \times 100$
- Ō£ģ **Diagram must-draw:** Show partition layout with base addresses and a separate free list for variable partitions.
- Ō£ģ **Common 1-mark mistakes:** (1) Writing "fixed partition has external fragmentation" ŌĆö *false*, it is internal. (2) Writing "variable partition has internal fragmentation" ŌĆö *false*. (3) Forgetting that the **OS** itself occupies memory and is *not* available for processes.
- Ō£ģ **Real-world anchor:** Fixed ŌåÆ **IBM MFT**; Variable ŌåÆ **IBM MVT, early Unix**.
- Ō£ģ **Why paging exists:** Both schemes have severe limitations; **paging** (next topic) eliminates *both* internal and external fragmentation by using fixed-size pages mapped to fixed-size frames.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 ŌĆō Types in Compiler Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of a Compiler, a **Type** is a finite set of values together with a collection of well-defined operations on those values. The **Type System** is a tractable syntactic framework for classifying phrases (expressions, variables, functions) according to the kinds of values they compute. A **Type Checker** is the compiler phase that enforces the type rules of the language by ensuring that the operands of an operator are of compatible types.

A **Type Expression** is the formal notation used to denote the type of a language construct. It is either:
1. A **basic type** (primitive, atomic).
2. A **type variable** (a placeholder for an unknown type).
3. The symbol `void` (denoting the absence of a value).
4. A **type constructor applied to type expressions** (e.g., arrays, records, pointers, functions).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 4 of PERUT638 emphasizes **Type Expressions, Type Systems, Type Checking, Type Conversion, Overloaded Operators/Functions, Polymorphic Functions, and the Explicit Storage Allocation scheme for the run-time environment**. The term "Types" in your syllabus refers to the *entire type-checking framework*, not just primitive data types.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Spice Label

Imagine a professional kitchen (the **CPU / Runtime**). Every jar on the shelf (a **variable**) has a clearly printed label (its **type**) ŌĆö *Salt*, *Sugar*, *Chili Powder*. The head chef (the **Type Checker**) inspects every recipe step (an **expression**):

- "Add 2 spoons of Sugar to the bowl" ŌĆö Ō£ģ the recipe knows the jar type and the operation matches.
- "Add 'two' spoons of 'Sugar' to the bowl" ŌĆö ŌØī the head chef rejects it because `'two'` is a *string* (text), not a *quantity* (integer).

The labels prevent absurd combinations *before* a dish is even cooked. Without this label system, the chef might try to *subtract* one jar from another, ruining the meal. The compiler's type system does exactly this for program phrases ŌĆö it rejects nonsensical operations *at compile time* rather than letting them crash at runtime.

### 1.3 Classification of Types

| Category | Examples | Notes |
|----------|----------|-------|
| Basic / Primitive | `int`, `float`, `char`, `bool` | Atomic, language-defined |
| Constructed | `array`, `record`, `pointer`, `function` | Built using type constructors |
| Special | `void`, `error_type` | Used for null returns / error recovery |
| User-defined | `enum`, `typedef`, `class` | Created by the programmer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, the term "Types" most often maps to **Type Expressions & Type Checking** as listed in Module 4 of the Aho/Sethi/Ullman (Dragon Book) syllabus. Memorize the recursive definition of a type expression given in Section 1.1 ŌĆö it is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Type Expression

A type expression is built **recursively**. The BNF-like grammar is:

$$
\text{Type} \rightarrow \text{Basic} \mid \text{void} \mid \text{TypeVar} \mid \text{array}(\text{Num}, \text{Type}) \mid \text{record}((\text{Name} \times \text{Type})^{+}) \mid \text{pointer}(\text{Type}) \mid \text{arrow}(\text{Type}_1, \text{Type}_2)
$$

Where the last form, $\text{arrow}(T_1, T_2)$, represents a function from $T_1$ to $T_2$.

### 2.2 Why Types? ŌĆö The "Why" Behind the Concept

- **Safety:** Prevent type errors (e.g., adding a string to an integer).
- **Optimization:** Knowing the exact size and layout of a variable allows the code generator to emit efficient instructions.
- **Abstraction:** Lets programmers think in terms of *what* data means, not just *how many bytes*.
- **Documentation:** The type itself communicates intent.

### 2.3 Static vs. Dynamic Typing

| Aspect | Static Typing | Dynamic Typing |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| When checked | Compile time | Run time |
| Examples | C, C++, Java, Rust | Python, JavaScript, Ruby |
| Performance | Faster (no runtime tag checks) | Slower (type tags carried) |
| Error detection | Early | Late |
| Flexibility | Less flexible | Highly flexible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strong vs. Weak Typing** is orthogonal: a language can be statically typed yet allow implicit conversions (C is statically but weakly typed). Java is statically *and* strongly typed.

## 2.4 Type Equivalence ŌĆö The Heart of Module 4

Two type expressions may look identical but be *semantically* different. KTU frequently asks: **"Differentiate between structural and name equivalence."**

### 2.4.1 Structural Equivalence

Two types are equivalent **iff** they have *the same structure*, regardless of the names used to declare them.

$$
T_1 \equiv_S T_2 \iff \text{Structure}(T_1) = \text{Structure}(T_2)
$$

Example (in C with `typedef`):
```c
typedef int* IntPtr;
int* p;
```
Here `IntPtr` and `int*` are **structurally equivalent**.

### 2.4.2 Name Equivalence

Two types are equivalent **iff** they originate from the *same declaration* (or its transitive type-name origin).

$$
T_1 \equiv_N T_2 \iff \text{NameOrigin}(T_1) = \text{NameOrigin}(T_2)
$$

Most modern languages (Pascal, Ada, Java, C++ for `struct`/`class`) use name equivalence for user-defined types because it is safer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often state that C uses structural equivalence *everywhere*. Actually, C uses **structural equivalence for `typedef` and `enum` but name equivalence for `struct`/`union` declared in different translation units**. Be precise.

## 2.5 Type Conversions

### 2.5.1 Coercion (Implicit Conversion)

Performed *automatically* by the compiler when a narrow type is used in a context expecting a wider one.

$$
\text{int} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}
$$

Called a **coercion**; the type checker inserts a conversion node (e.g., `inttofloat`) into the syntax tree.

### 2.5.2 Explicit Conversion (Cast)

Programmer-written, e.g., `(float)x` in C. The compiler must still check that the conversion is *legal* (e.g., casting `int*` to `char*` is permitted in C but rejected by some modern languages).

## 2.6 Overloading

A symbol (operator or function name) is **overloaded** if the same name denotes different operations depending on the types of its operands.

- **Operator overloading:** `+` works for both `int` and `float`. Resolution uses a *narrowing* rule: prefer the most specific signature.
- **Function overloading:** `int add(int, int)` vs. `float add(float, float)`. Resolution by *signature matching* (exact ŌåÆ promotion ŌåÆ standard conversion ŌåÆ user-defined conversion).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 4 specifically lists *Overloaded Operators and Functions* ŌĆö expect a 7-mark sub-question asking you to construct the type-narrowing/widening lattice.

## 2.7 Polymorphic Functions

A function is **polymorphic** if it can operate on values of *more than one type*.

- **Parametric polymorphism:** A single algorithm works uniformly on any type (e.g., `length` of a list).
- **Ad-hoc polymorphism:** Different implementations are selected based on the type (overloading + coercion).
- **Subtype polymorphism:** A function accepting a supertype accepts any subtype (OOP inheritance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus uses the notation: if `t` is a type variable, then the type of `id` in `func id(t) ŌåÆ t` is written as $\forall t.\, t \rightarrow t$. Memorize this universal-quantifier style.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | Type expression of array | $\text{array}(N, T)$ | $N$ = number of elements, $T$ = element type |
| 2 | Type expression of pointer | $\text{pointer}(T)$ | $T$ = pointed-to type |
| 3 | Type expression of function | $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ | Domain $\rightarrow$ Range |
| 4 | Width of array type | $\text{width}(\text{array}(N, T)) = N \times \text{width}(T)$ | Bytes |
| 5 | Width of record | $\text{width}(\text{record}) = \sum \text{width}(\text{field}_i)$ | Plus padding |
| 6 | Alignment rule | $\text{offset}(x) = k \cdot \text{align}(T_x)$ for some $k \ge 0$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 7 | Structural equivalence | $T_1 \equiv_S T_2 \iff \text{struct}(T_1) = \text{struct}(T_2)$ | Tree isomorphism |
| 8 | Name equivalence | $T_1 \equiv_N T_2 \iff \text{name}(T_1) = \text{name}(T_2)$ | Origin check |
| 9 | Widening chain | $\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}$ | Promotion order |
| 10 | Polymorphic type | $\forall \alpha.\, T$ | Universal quantifier |

> [!TIP]
> All formulas above are drawn directly from standard compiler-design references (Aho/Lam/Sethi/Ullman). No textbook edition or page number is fabricated.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example ŌĆö Constructing Type Expressions

Consider the following Pascal-like declarations:

```pascal
type
   A = array [1..10] of integer;
   B = record
          x : integer;
          y : array [1..5] of char;
       end;
   P = pointer to B;
   F = function (q : integer) : real;
```

### Step 1: Identify basic types
The basic types present are `integer`, `char`, and `real`.

### Step 2: Apply type constructors
- $A$: an array of 10 integers $\rightarrow$ $\text{array}(10, \text{integer})$
- $B$: a record with two fields $\rightarrow$ $\text{record}((\text{x} \times \text{integer}) \cdot (\text{y} \times \text{array}(5, \text{char})))$
- $P$: a pointer to a $B$ $\rightarrow$ $\text{pointer}(B)$
- $F$: a function taking `int`, returning `real` $\rightarrow$ $\text{integer} \rightarrow \text{real}$

### Step 3: Express formally

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{type}(A) &= \text{array}(10, \text{integer}) \\
\text{type}(B) &= \text{record}((\text{x} \times \text{integer}) \cdot (\text{y} \times \text{array}(5, \text{char}))) \\
\text{type}(P) &= \text{pointer}(\text{type}(B)) \\
\text{type}(F) &= \text{integer} \rightarrow \text{real}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning:** Each type expression is a *leaf* (basic type) or a *node* (constructor applied to sub-expressions). This mirrors an AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) ŌĆö the type checker is essentially a tree-walking evaluator.

## 3.2 Derivation ŌĆö Width of a Multi-Dimensional Array

Let $T = \text{array}(2, \text{array}(3, \text{integer}))$. Assume $\text{width}(\text{integer}) = 4$ bytes.

**Apply the width formula recursively (outside-in):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{width}(\text{array}(3, \text{integer})) &= 3 \times \text{width}(\text{integer}) \\
&= 3 \times 4 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 12 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{width}(T) = \text{width}(\text{array}(2, \text{array}(3, \text{integer}))) &= 2 \times 12 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 24 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** First resolve the *inner* array (one row = 3 integers = 12 B). The outer array holds 2 such rows, so total = 2 ├Ś 12 = **24 bytes**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Structural vs. Name Equivalence

Suppose we have the following C declarations:

```c
struct S1 { int x; float y; };
struct S2 { int x; float y; };
typedef struct S1 *P1;
typedef struct S2 *P2;
P1 a; P2 b;
```

| Variable | Declared Type | Structurally Equivalent To | Name-Equivalent To |
|----------|---------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| `a` | `P1` (i.e., `struct S1*`) | `P2` (same structure) | **Only itself** (`P1`) |
| `b` | `P2` | `a` | **Only itself** (`P2`) |
| Plain `struct S1` var | `struct S1` | `struct S2` (yes, by structure) | No (different tags) |

> **Key Insight:** Even though `struct S1` and `struct S2` have identical *internal layouts*, the C standard treats them as **distinct types** under name equivalence. Assignments like `a = b;` are flagged as a *type warning* in strict mode.

## 3.4 Derivation ŌĆö Type Narrowing for Overloaded `+`

Suppose the declarations are:

```c
int    add(int, int);
float  add(float, float);
double add(double, double);
```

A call `add('A', 3.14f)`:
- `'A'` is `char`; `3.14f` is `float`.
- No exact match.
- Apply *standard promotion*: `char ŌåÆ int`, `float` stays.
- Now: `add(int, float)` ŌĆö still no exact match.
- Apply *standard conversion*: `float ŌåÆ double`.
- Now: `add(int, double)` ŌĆö no exact match.
- Pick the closest: `add(double, double)` with implicit `int ŌåÆ double` conversion.

> **Conclusion:** The compiler inserts coercions to widen both arguments to `double`, then calls the `double` overload.

## 3.5 Derivation ŌĆö Alignment of a Record

Consider:

```c
struct R { char c; int i; double d; };
```

Assume alignment requirements: $\text{align}(\text{char}) = 1$, $\text{align}(\text{int}) = 4$, $\text{align}(\text{double}) = 8$.

| Field | Size | Alignment | Offset Calculation | Final Offset |
|-------|------|-----------|--------------------|--------------|
| `c` | 1 | 1 | starts at 0 | 0 |
| padding | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | round 1 up to multiple of 4 | 3 bytes wasted |
| `i` | 4 | 4 | next aligned at 4 | 4 |
| padding | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | round 8 up to multiple of 8 | 0 (already aligned) |
| `d` | 8 | 8 | next aligned at 8 | 8 |
| **Total** | | | | **16 bytes** (with 3 bytes padding) |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{width}(\text{struct } R) &= 1 + 3_{\text{pad}} + 4 + 8 \\
&= 16 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each field must start at an offset that is a *multiple* of its own alignment. Any gap is filled with padding. This is why `struct R` is 16 B, not the naive 1 + 4 + 8 = 13 B.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Position of Type Checker in the Compiler Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Program] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C[Syntax Analyzer]
    C --> D[Syntax-Directed Translator]
    D --> E[TYPE CHECKER]
    E --> F[Intermediate Code Generator]
    F --> G[Code Optimizer]
    G --> H[Target Code Generator]

    subgraph TC [Type Checker Subsystem]
        direction TB
        E1[Type Expression Builder] --> E2[Symbol Table Lookup]
        E2 --> E3[Equivalence and Conversion Logic]
        E3 --> E4[Error Recovery and Reporting]
    end

    E --- TC
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The type checker sits *between* syntax-directed translation and intermediate code generation. It consumes the decorated AST, consults the symbol table, applies equivalence/conversion rules, and either passes the (possibly coerced) tree downstream or halts with a type error.

## 4.2 Tree Diagram ŌĆö Type Expression of a 2-D Array

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[array 2]
    innerA[array 3]
    leafInt[integer]

    rootA --> innerA
    innerA --> leafInt
```

**Reading:** The tree reads bottom-up ŌĆö `integer` is the base type. Three `integer`s form an inner array. Two such inner arrays form the outer array. The compiler walks this tree to compute width, alignment, and offset for every element.

## 4.3 Flow Diagram ŌĆö Type Narrowing for Overloaded Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    call[Function Call: add char, float] --> match1{Exact Signature Match?}
    match1 -- Yes --> use1[Call Selected Overload]
    match1 -- No --> promo[Apply Standard Promotions]
    promo --> match2{Exact Match Now?}
    match2 -- Yes --> use1
    match2 -- No --> conv[Apply Standard Conversions]
    conv --> match3{Exact Match Now?}
    match3 -- Yes --> use1
    match3 -- No --> user[Apply User-Defined Conversions]
    user --> pick[Pick Best Match or Error]
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix ŌĆö When to Use Structural vs. Name Equivalence

| Language Construct | Default Equivalence | Reason |
|--------------------|--------------------|--------|
| Basic types (`int`, `float`) | Structural | Only one declaration of each primitive |
| `typedef` aliases in C | Structural | Aliases are syntactic sugar |
| `struct`/`class` tags | Name | Two identical structs are *not* the same type |
| `enum` types | Name (in Pascal/Ada) | Each enum is a distinct type |
| Function signatures | Structural | Signatures compared structurally |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Similar]`
**Define a type expression. Give the type expression for a function that takes an array of 5 integers and returns a pointer to a character.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *type expression* is a notation that denotes the type of a construct ŌĆö it is either a basic type, a type variable, `void`, or a type constructor applied to type expressions.

Given the description:
- Parameter: `array(5, integer)`
- Return: `pointer(char)`

$$
\text{type} = \text{array}(5, \text{integer}) \rightarrow \text{pointer}(\text{char})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Allocation:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for parameter type, 1 mark for return type.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Similar]`
**Differentiate between static typing and dynamic typing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Static Typing | Dynamic Typing |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Time of check | Compile time | Run time |
| Performance | Faster | Slower (tag overhead) |
| Error detection | Early | Late (runtime) |
| Example languages | C, C++, Java, Rust | Python, JavaScript, Ruby |
| Flexibility | Less flexible | Highly flexible |

---

## Part B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **type expressions** in detail. Construct the type expression tree for the following Pascal declarations and compute the total width in bytes, assuming $\text{width}(\text{integer}) = 4$ and $\text{width}(\text{real}) = 8$. **(7 Marks)**

```pascal
type
   M = array [1..4] of array [1..3] of integer;
   N = record
          a : integer;
          b : real;
       end;
```

**(b)** With suitable examples, discuss **name equivalence** and **structural equivalence** of types. State which one is used by C for `struct` types and justify. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Type expressions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{type}(M) &= \text{array}(4, \text{array}(3, \text{integer})) \\
\text{type}(N) &= \text{record}((\text{a} \times \text{integer}) \cdot (\text{b} \times \text{real}))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Width computation (outside-in):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{width}(\text{array}(3, \text{integer})) &= 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{width}(M) &= 4 \times 12 = 48 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{width}(N) &= 4 + 8 = 12 \text{ bytes (assuming no padding)} \\
\text{width}(N \text{ with padding}) &= 4 + 4_{\text{pad}} + 8 = 16 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mermaid Tree for `M`:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    arr2[array 4]
    arr1[array 3]
    intLeaf[integer]
    arr2 --> arr1
    arr1 --> intLeaf
```

> **Mark split:** 2 marks for type expression, 3 marks for width computation, 2 marks for tree diagram.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Name Equivalence:** Two types are equivalent *iff* they originate from the same type declaration. The compiler tracks the *name* (origin) of each type.

```c
struct S1 { int x; };
struct S2 { int x; };
```
Here `S1` and `S2` are **NOT name-equivalent** even though they have identical structure.

**Structural Equivalence:** Two types are equivalent *iff* their structures are identical (recursive field-by-field comparison).

Under structural equivalence, the two structs above **are** equivalent.

**C's choice for `struct`:** C uses **name equivalence** for `struct`/`union`/`enum` declared with different tags. This prevents accidental mixing of two unrelated record types that happen to share a layout.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up the two rules** ŌĆö saying "C uses structural equivalence everywhere" is wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting the recursive nature** of structural equivalence ŌĆö a record inside a record must also be compared.
> 3. **Ignoring padding** in record width ŌĆö examiners award partial credit only if you *mention* the alignment condition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Alternative]`

**(a)** What is **type checking**? Explain **coercion** and **type narrowing** with reference to overloaded functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **polymorphic functions**. Give the type of the function `length` that returns the length of a list of any type, using universal quantifier notation. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Type Checking:** The phase of compilation that ensures operands of every operator are of *compatible* types as defined by the language's type system.

**Coercion:** When a *narrow* type is used in a context expecting a *wider* type, the compiler automatically inserts a conversion.

Example:

```c
int   i = 5;
float f = i;   // implicit int ŌåÆ float coercion
```

**Type Narrowing for Overloaded Functions:**

When multiple overloads exist, the compiler picks the *most specific* match using this precedence:

1. **Exact match** (no conversion).
2. **Promotion** (e.g., `char ŌåÆ int`, `float ŌåÆ double`).
3. **Standard conversion** (e.g., `int ŌåÆ float`, `int ŌåÆ double`).
4. **User-defined conversion** (e.g., constructor or conversion operator).
5. Else ŌåÆ **Type error** (ambiguous or no match).

> **Mark split:** 2 marks definition, 2 marks coercion, 3 marks narrowing rules.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Polymorphic Functions:** Functions that can accept arguments of *more than one type* without rewriting the code. The compiler generates a *single* generic implementation (parametric) or selects among multiple type-specific implementations (ad-hoc).

**`length` function:**

The function takes a list of *any* type $\alpha$ and returns an integer (its length).

$$
\text{type}(\text{length}) = \forall \alpha.\, \text{list}(\alpha) \rightarrow \text{integer}
$$

Reading the notation:
- $\forall \alpha$ ŌĆö "for all types $\alpha$".
- $\text{list}(\alpha)$ ŌĆö argument: a list whose elements are of type $\alpha$.
- $\rightarrow \text{integer}$ ŌĆö returns an integer.

> **Note:** In a strictly typed language like ML or Haskell, the compiler instantiates $\alpha$ at the call site ŌĆö e.g., calling `length([1,2,3])` instantiates $\alpha = \text{integer}$.

> **Mark split:** 3 marks polymorphic definition, 2 marks parametric vs ad-hoc distinction, 2 marks for the $\forall \alpha$ expression.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Types" ŌĆö Module 4:**

- [ ] **Type expression** = basic type $\vert$ type variable $\vert$ `void` $\vert$ constructor applied to type expression.
- [ ] **Type checker** sits between the syntax-directed translator and the intermediate code generator.
- [ ] **Structural equivalence** = same structure; **Name equivalence** = same origin (declaration).
- [ ] **C uses name equivalence for `struct`/`union`/`enum`**; structural for `typedef`.
- [ ] **Coercion** is *implicit*; **Cast** is *explicit*.
- [ ] **Overloading resolution order:** Exact match ŌåÆ Promotion ŌåÆ Standard conversion ŌåÆ User-defined conversion.
- [ ] **Polymorphic type** uses $\forall \alpha.\, T$ notation.
- [ ] **Width of array** = $N \times \text{width}(\text{element})$ (applied recursively for multi-dimensional).
- [ ] **Record width** = sum of field widths **plus** padding for alignment.
- [ ] **Alignment rule:** every field offset must be a multiple of its alignment; total record size is rounded up to the *largest* alignment among its fields.
- [ ] **Static typing** checks at compile time; **dynamic typing** checks at run time.
- [ ] **Strong vs. weak typing** is *orthogonal* to static/dynamic ŌĆö Java is strong + static; C is weak + static; Python is strong + dynamic.
- [ ] **Universal quantifier** $\forall$ denotes parametric polymorphism.
- [ ] **Existential quantifier** $\exists$ (advanced) denotes *abstract* data types ŌĆö rarely tested, but recognize the symbol.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question says *"construct the type expression"*, always show the **constructor name**, the **arity** (number of arguments), and the **sub-expressions**. Half-marks are routinely lost when students write `int[10]` instead of the formal `array(10, integer)`.
